title: Formalizing Latent Thoughts: Four Axioms of Thought Representation in LLMs
summary: We introduce an axiomatic evaluation framework for latent thought representations in LLMs, comprising metrics that are independent of downstream benchmark scores and reveal representational failures that benchmark accuracy masks. Existing evaluations conflate representation quality with model capacity. Therefore, failures cannot be attributed to the representation rather than to the model that processes it. We formalize four functional axioms (Causality, Minimality, Separability, and Stability) and define a quantitative measure for each, computed directly on the representation independently of downstream accuracy. We audit open-weight LLMs across 23 reasoning tasks (e.g., Spatial Reasoning, Factual QA). We find that no candidate satisfies all four axioms simultaneously, that the representations distinguish task type reliably but cannot distinguish between two questions within the same task, and that the representations encode little information beyond what is already present in the input embedding. The failure is consistent across dense, reasoning-distilled, and RL-trained model families, indicating that the gap is structural rather than a property of model size or training procedure.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an axiomatic, task-agnostic framework to evaluate latent thought representations in large language models, showing that current models systematically fail to produce robust, informative reasoning representations even when they perform well on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of LLM 'reasoning' rely on downstream benchmark accuracy, which conflates how well the model represents intermediate thoughts with how well it can use those representations to generate answers. This makes it impossible to know whether observed failures arise from poor representations or from limitations in decoding. The authors want a principled, model-agnostic way to assess the quality and functionality of latent thought representations themselves, to uncover hidden weaknesses that benchmarks gloss over.
AI.method: The authors define four functional axioms that a good latent thought representation should satisfy: Causality (the representation should causally affect outputs), Minimality (it should not contain superfluous information beyond what is needed for the task), Separability (it should distinguish different inputs, e.g., questions, in a task-relevant way), and Stability (it should be robust to small perturbations). For each axiom they design a quantitative metric that can be computed directly on the internal representation layer, without using task accuracy. They then extract latent thought representations from various open-weight LLMs across 23 reasoning tasks, and compute these metrics to audit how well each model’s representations satisfy the axioms.
AI.result: Across all tested open-weight LLMs and 23 reasoning tasks, no model’s latent thought representation satisfies all four axioms simultaneously. Representations reliably encode task-type information (e.g., whether an example is spatial reasoning or factual QA) but cannot reliably distinguish between individual questions within the same task. Analyses also show that these reasoning representations add little information beyond what is already present in the original input embeddings. These patterns are observed consistently across different model families, including dense models, models distilled for reasoning, and models trained with reinforcement learning.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current LLM latent 'thought' representations exhibit structural deficiencies: they fail to meet key functional axioms needed for robust, task-specific reasoning, and mostly repackage information already in the input rather than constructing rich, discriminative internal thoughts. Since these failures are consistent across architectures, sizes, and training schemes, the gap appears structural, not just an artifact of scale or fine-tuning. The authors argue that future work should explicitly target improving the structure and functionality of latent reasoning representations, rather than only optimizing benchmark accuracy.

title: Position: The Term "Machine Unlearning" Is Overused in LLMs
comment: 13 pages; ICML 2026 Position Paper Track. Sangyeon Yoon and Yeachan Jun contributed equally
summary: Large language models increasingly face demands to "forget" training data, knowledge, or behaviors due to regulatory deletion obligations, copyright/licensing disputes, and safety or product-policy requirements. This position paper argues that machine unlearning is overused as a term in LLM research and should be reserved for dataset-defined deletion: removing the training influence of a precisely specified forget set such that the resulting model is approximately indistinguishable from retraining without that data. We contend that many tasks currently labeled "unlearning" (e.g., refusal for harmful requests, entity/knowledge removal, or targeted suppression) pursue different, often policy-dependent objectives and therefore require different terminology and baselines (e.g., alignment, suppression, editing, obfuscation). We further argue that this confusion is not cosmetic: because papers make different implicit guarantees under the same label, metrics and benchmarks are frequently reused outside their intended scope, rewarding surface-level non-disclosure (e.g., low ROUGE/forget accuracy) even when retraining-equivalence is not tested and derived capabilities remain. We conclude by calling for stricter terminology tied to explicit guarantees and reference models, and for evaluations that match the claimed objective.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that the term "machine unlearning" is misused in LLM research and should be reserved for the strict case of removing the influence of a specific dataset so the model approximates retraining without it. Many current "unlearning" tasks are actually different objectives (like alignment or editing) and need distinct terminology and evaluation.
AI.motivation: There are growing legal, ethical, and safety pressures to make LLMs "forget" certain data or behaviors, but the community uses the term "unlearning" inconsistently. This creates confusion about what guarantees are provided, leads to misuse of benchmarks and metrics, and may falsely suggest strong data-deletion properties where only behavioral changes are achieved.
AI.method: The paper is a position/conceptual analysis rather than an empirical study. It defines a stricter notion of machine unlearning tied to dataset-defined deletion and approximate equivalence to retraining. It then categorizes various current "unlearning"-labeled tasks (e.g., safety refusals, knowledge removal, suppression) and argues they should instead be framed under different terms with appropriate baselines and guarantees.
AI.result: The paper conceptually separates true dataset-level unlearning from policy-driven behavior changes, showing that many existing works conflate these. It highlights that popular evaluation metrics (like forget-set accuracy or text similarity) can be gamed by superficial non-disclosure while leaving internal representations and derived capabilities intact, and that these metrics are often misapplied across differing objectives.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the field needs stricter, guarantee-oriented terminology: "unlearning" should be reserved for dataset-defined, retraining-equivalent deletion, while other objectives (alignment, suppression, editing, obfuscation) should be labeled as such. They call for evaluations that are aligned with the claimed objective and tied to explicit reference models, to avoid overclaiming and to make progress on genuinely reliable data deletion and behavior control.

title: A Survey of Automated Presentation Coaching: Systems, Methods, and Open Challenges
comment: accepted into the BEA 2026 workshop at ACL
summary: Automated coaching for oral presentations sits at the intersection of computer-assisted pronunciation training (CAPT), prosody modeling, and speech synthesis, yet no prior work has systematically surveyed and compared existing systems along these dimensions. This survey reviews and categorizes automated presentation coaching systems, spanning pronunciation tutors, fluency and prosody coaches, multimodal trainers, and conference Q&A practice tools. We introduce a five-dimensional task taxonomy - covering segmental pronunciation, lexical stress, suprasegmental prosody, pacing, and content faithfulness - and explicitly map surveyed systems onto it to reveal coverage gaps. We further review the core technical methods these systems employ: TTS-based exemplar generation and diagnostic methods for pronunciation, prosody, and fluency assessment. Key open challenges include the scarcity of annotated presentation corpora, achieving accent-fair feedback across diverse L1 backgrounds, and delivering low-latency diagnostics for real-time rehearsal.

AI.tldr: Survey of automated coaching tools for oral presentations, proposing a taxonomy and highlighting gaps and challenges.
AI.motivation: There is growing interest in automated help for oral presentations, but existing systems span different traditions (CAPT, prosody coaching, TTS tools) and have not been systematically compared. A unified view is needed to understand what capabilities exist, where gaps are, and what technical challenges remain.
AI.method: Conduct a systematic survey of automated presentation coaching systems, categorize them into functional types (pronunciation tutors, prosody and fluency coaches, multimodal trainers, Q&A practice tools), and propose a five-dimensional task taxonomy: segmental pronunciation, lexical stress, suprasegmental prosody, pacing, and content faithfulness. Map existing systems to this taxonomy and review underlying technical methods such as TTS-based exemplars and diagnostic algorithms for pronunciation, prosody, and fluency.
AI.result: A structured categorization of existing systems is provided, together with a mapping of each system to the five-dimensional taxonomy, which reveals underexplored areas. The survey also synthesizes the main technical approaches used for exemplar generation and automated assessment across pronunciation, prosody, and fluency.
AI.conclusion: Current automated presentation coaching systems only partially cover the space of desirable feedback dimensions, with notable gaps and limitations in data, fairness, and latency. Future work should collect richer annotated presentation corpora, design methods that provide equitable feedback across accents, and develop fast diagnostic models suitable for real-time rehearsal support.

title: Causal Connections: Leveraging Multilingual Fine-Tuning for Financial QA@FinCausal 2026
summary: This paper describes team HSA_CORAL's submission to the FinCausal 2026 shared task on extracting cause-effect relations from financial narratives via extractive question answering in English and Spanish. We compare three modeling families: (i) encoder-only token tagging with multilingual BERT, (ii) encoder-decoder generation with multilingual BART, and (iii) decoder-only LLMs (Llama 3.1 and GPT variants) using prompt refinement, few-shot demonstrations, and supervised fine-tuning. Across settings, prompting and few-shot examples yield competitive performance, while supervised fine-tuning provides the largest gains. Our best system, GPT-4.1 Mini fine-tuned on combined English and Spanish training data, achieves a tied highest score on the English subtask (score 4.8140) and ranks third on Spanish (score 4.7753) under the shared task's LLM-as-a-judge metric. Overall, the results highlight the value of task-specific adaptation and multilingual fine-tuning for cross-lingual transfer in financial causality QA.

AI.tldr: They evaluate different model families for extracting cause-effect relations in financial text in English and Spanish, finding that supervised fine-tuning of a multilingual GPT-4.1 Mini model performs best and demonstrates strong cross-lingual transfer.
AI.motivation: To improve automatic extraction of cause-effect relations from financial narratives, a key task for understanding financial documents, by systematically comparing modeling approaches and exploring multilingual, cross-lingual setups.
AI.method: They frame financial causality extraction as extractive question answering in English and Spanish and compare: (i) encoder-only token tagging with multilingual BERT, (ii) encoder-decoder generation with multilingual BART, and (iii) decoder-only LLMs (Llama 3.1 and GPT variants) using prompt design, few-shot examples, and supervised fine-tuning. Models are trained and evaluated on the FinCausal 2026 shared task data and scored with an LLM-as-a-judge metric.
AI.result: Prompted and few-shot LLMs already perform competitively, but supervised fine-tuning yields the largest performance boost. The best system is GPT-4.1 Mini fine-tuned on the combined English and Spanish data, which ties for first place in English (score 4.8140) and places third in Spanish (score 4.7753) on the shared task leaderboard.
AI.conclusion: Task-specific adaptation and multilingual fine-tuning substantially improve financial causality question answering, demonstrating effective cross-lingual transfer and showing that small decoder-only LLMs can be strong contenders in specialized financial NLP tasks.

title: AI-Model Network: Concept, Current State and Future
summary: While the primary function of computers lies in computation and processing, the core value of the Internet is rooted in sharing and collaboration. Computers create the Internet, and the Internet empowers the value of computers. The rapid development of the Internet, cloud computing, and big data is pushing artificial intelligence into the era of large models (LMs). However, the practical application of LMs is currently hindered by high training costs and deployment complexities, driving a shift toward lightweight, private, and domain-specific models. With the rapid proliferation and wide distribution of heterogeneous models, enabling effective interaction and collaboration among them has emerged as a critical bottleneck that urgently needs to be addressed in LM development. Drawing inspiration from the development of the Internet, this paper proposes the concept, vision, and system architecture of world wide AI-model network (AI-ModelNet). It is a novel paradigm that achieves interconnection, capability sharing, and collaborative reasoning by establishing pathways between models. We first briefly review the current state of single-model and multi-model research. Subsequently, the systemic vision and hierarchical architecture of AI-ModelNet are articulated, followed by validation of the framework's feasibility through a prototype system and diverse application cases. Finally, key directions for future research are discussed preliminarily.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AI-ModelNet, a networked paradigm for connecting heterogeneous AI models so they can share capabilities and collaborate, validated by a prototype and example applications.
AI.motivation: Large models are powerful but expensive to train and deploy, and the field is moving toward many lightweight, private, domain-specific models. As these heterogeneous models proliferate, the lack of mechanisms for them to interact and collaborate becomes a major bottleneck, analogous to pre-Internet isolated computers. The paper is motivated by the need for an Internet-like infrastructure that connects models and allows them to interoperate.
AI.method: The authors conceptually design and formalize AI-ModelNet: they (1) survey existing single-model and multi-model research to situate their work; (2) propose a systemic vision and hierarchical architecture for an AI-model network that enables interconnection, capability sharing, and collaborative reasoning; and (3) build a prototype system and demonstrate it on multiple application cases to validate basic feasibility.
AI.result: They define the concept and layered architecture of AI-ModelNet and demonstrate, through a prototype and various use cases, that heterogeneous models can be interconnected to share capabilities and collaborate on tasks. These results show that the AI-ModelNet paradigm is technically feasible and can support practical applications across scenarios, though performance details are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that an Internet-like AI-ModelNet is a promising paradigm for overcoming the isolation of numerous heterogeneous models, enabling interconnection, capability sharing, and collaborative reasoning at scale. The prototype and applications offer initial evidence of feasibility, and the authors outline several key research directions needed to fully realize this vision in the future.

title: Developmental approach reveals the statistical learning of Neural Language Models: Transformers generalize from the most abstract statistical patterns
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures, oral presentation at Interdisciplinary Advances in Statistical Learning
summary: In this study, we use a developmental approach to investigate the statistical learning and mental representation of neural language models (NLM). A series of Generative Transformer models are trained on a synthetic grammar. The model states are saved at multiple stages in the course of training. Through analyzing how the internal representations of these models change in the developmental path, we found that NLMs acquire the most abstract global statistical knowledge at the beginning of learning and later acquire the relatively local statistical dependencies. This learning path contains many over-generalizations from the very beginning and these over-generalizations are gradually constrained in the later stage of learning. Based on this observation, we propose a new framework to explain the statistical learning and language cognition of NLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how neural language models (NLMs) learn over time by tracking their internal representations while training on a synthetic grammar.
AI.motivation: To understand the developmental trajectory of statistical learning in NLMs and how their internal representations of grammatical structure evolve, analogous to developmental studies in human language learning.
AI.method: Train a series of generative Transformer models on a controlled synthetic grammar, save model states (checkpoints) at multiple training stages, and analyze changes in internal representations across these checkpoints.
AI.result: NLMs first acquire abstract, global statistical patterns of the grammar, and only later learn more local statistical dependencies. Early in training, they exhibit broad over-generalizations that gradually become constrained as training proceeds.
AI.conclusion: The authors propose a new framework for explaining statistical learning and language cognition in NLMs, grounded in the observed developmental path from early abstract generalizations to later refined local dependencies and reduced over-generalization.

title: When Does Personality Composition Matter for Multi-Agent LLM Teams?
summary: Personality prompting shapes how large language models communicate, yet whether these behavioral shifts affect objective task outcomes remains under-explored. Prior work shows that agents prompted with low agreeableness produce adversarial language, while those prompted with high agreeableness become cooperative, but the relationship between communication style and task performance has not been systematically examined across multiple domains. In this work, we investigate whether personality composition matters for multi-agent team performance by manipulating personality traits across frontier LLMs on three task domains: structured coding, open-ended research collaboration, and competitive bargaining. We find that personality effects depend critically on task structure. In coding tasks, low agreeableness leads to large communication shifts that have little effect on milestone completion. In open-ended collaboration and bargaining, the same manipulation substantially degrades performance. We discuss implications for multi-agent system design and the limits of personality manipulation.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how personality-based prompting of large language model (LLM) agents affects their communication style and, more importantly, their performance on different multi-agent tasks.
AI.motivation: While personality prompts are known to change how LLMs talk (e.g., being more adversarial or more cooperative), it is unclear whether these stylistic changes meaningfully influence objective task outcomes across diverse domains. The authors aim to bridge this gap, especially for multi-agent systems where team composition and coordination matter.
AI.method: The authors prompt frontier LLMs with different personality traits—particularly varying levels of agreeableness—and form multi-agent teams. They then evaluate these teams on three domains: (1) structured coding tasks with clear milestones, (2) open-ended research-style collaboration, and (3) competitive bargaining scenarios. They systematically compare communication patterns and task performance across personality configurations and domains.
AI.result: In coding tasks, changing agreeableness strongly alters communication style but has little effect on whether milestones are completed. In contrast, in open-ended collaboration and bargaining tasks, lowering agreeableness significantly worsens team performance, indicating that the impact of personality depends heavily on the structure and openness of the task.
AI.conclusion: Personality prompting does not uniformly improve or degrade LLM team performance; its effects are task-dependent. For structured, well-specified tasks, personality-induced communication differences may not matter much for outcomes, but for open-ended or strategically interactive tasks, personality composition can substantially influence success. This has important implications for designing multi-agent LLM systems and shows limits to the benefits of naive personality manipulation.

title: Supersede: Diagnosing and Training the Memory-Update Gap in LLM Agents
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Code, environment, model, and dataset: https://github.com/Vrin-cloud/supersede
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents operate over long, multi-session interactions in which facts change: a user moves, a price updates, a plan is revised. Acting correctly requires using the current value of a fact and discarding values that have been superseded. We isolate this ability on real conversational data and show that it is a distinct, unsolved failure. On the knowledge-update subset of LongMemEval, replacing an agent's full context with a bounded, self-maintained memory drops accuracy from 92% to 77% even on a frontier model (gpt-5.4), a gap that is statistically significant (paired McNemar p<0.005) and persists across model scale while full-context accuracy saturates near 92%. The bottleneck is therefore memory maintenance, not comprehension, and is not closed by a stronger model. We then ask whether this is merely an undersized memory, and find it is not: as the conversation grows 24x, accuracy falls further (from 68% to 28%), and granting the agent proportionally more memory yields no detectable recovery (28% to 28%, n=25). The failure scales with the length of the conversation, not the compression ratio. We release Supersede, an open reinforcement-learning environment (on the verifiers / prime-rl stack) that turns this measurement into a training signal: agents are rewarded for answering from the current value and penalized for stale ones. Finally, we close the loop and show the gap is trainable: GRPO fine-tuning a small open model (Qwen2.5-3B) on this environment nearly doubles its held-out supersession accuracy on real, unseen conversations (9.0% to 16.7%, a single run), along a monotonic checkpoint curve indicating the learned policy, not the harness, carries the gain. To our knowledge this is the first trainable environment whose reward targets temporal fact-currency, and the first evidence the supersession gap can be trained down, not only measured.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and measures a distinct failure of LLM agents: keeping track of updated facts over long, multi-session conversations, and shows this “supersession gap” is due to memory maintenance rather than model comprehension. It introduces Supersede, a reinforcement-learning environment that rewards using up-to-date facts, and demonstrates that fine-tuning in this environment can measurably improve agents’ ability to use current information instead of stale data.
AI.motivation: LLM agents increasingly operate in long, evolving interactions where facts change over time (locations, prices, plans). Correct behavior requires using the latest value of each fact and discarding outdated ones. Existing benchmarks mostly check whether models can retrieve or reason over information given in context, but not whether they maintain and update a compact, self-selected memory as conversations grow. The authors observe that even strong models fail at this temporal fact-currency; hence they aim to isolate, quantify, and improve this specific capability.
AI.method: 1) Construct and use a “knowledge-update” subset of the LongMemEval benchmark, where the same fact is updated over time in realistic conversational data. 2) Compare agents that have full access to the entire conversation history with agents restricted to a bounded, self-maintained memory, across different model scales, to identify whether the bottleneck is comprehension or memory maintenance. 3) Systematically vary conversation length and memory size to distinguish failures due to memory capacity from those due to temporal maintenance strategy. 4) Build Supersede, an RL environment on the verifiers / prime-rl stack where agents are rewarded for answering with currently valid facts and penalized for using superseded ones. 5) Fine-tune a small open model (Qwen2.5-3B) using GRPO on this environment, track performance across checkpoints, and evaluate held-out supersession accuracy on unseen real conversations.
AI.result: 1) Using a bounded, self-maintained memory instead of full context on the knowledge-update subset of LongMemEval reduces accuracy from 92% to 77% for a frontier model (gpt-5.4), a statistically significant drop that remains across model sizes while full-context accuracy saturates near 92%. 2) When conversation length increases by 24×, accuracy with bounded memory drops further (from 68% to 28%), and proportionally increasing memory capacity does not improve performance (remaining at 28%, n=25). This shows performance degrades with conversation length, not with compression ratio. 3) In the Supersede RL environment, GRPO fine-tuning of Qwen2.5-3B nearly doubles held-out supersession accuracy from 9.0% to 16.7% on real unseen conversations, and the improvement follows a monotonic trend over checkpoints, indicating a genuine learned policy improvement rather than an artifact of the training harness.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that maintaining temporally correct, up-to-date facts in long, evolving interactions is a distinct and unresolved weakness of current LLM agents. The limitation stems from how agents maintain and update bounded memory, not from their underlying comprehension or model size, and it worsens with conversation length even when memory scale is increased. The authors provide Supersede as the first RL environment explicitly targeting temporal fact-currency and show initial evidence that this “supersession gap” is not only measurable but also trainable, as RL fine-tuning can improve agents’ use of current facts over stale ones. This opens a path toward systematically training agents that are robust to fact updates in long-term interactions.

title: Internalizing the Future: A Unified Agentic Training Paradigm for World Model Planning
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated strong capability in sequential decision-making, yet they remains fundamentally reactive in long-horizon tasks. Unlike humans who employ "what-if" reasoning to evaluate potential plans before commitment, standard agents lack an internal world model to simulate future outcomes. Therefore, we propose to internalize future-aware planning by training a single autoregressive model to verbalize both a prospective state rollout and a plan-conditioned success estimate-a textual analogue of the Q-value. Crucially, we identify a format-capability gap: simply fine-tuning agents on look-ahead traces during post-training leads to superficial mimicry of foresight without genuine predictive grounding. To bridge this gap, we introduce a three-stage training paradigm: (i) World Model Agentic Mid-Training (WM-AMT) to inject latent predictive capabilities into the policy; (ii) Format-Eliciting SFT (FE-SFT) to structure this injected capability; and (iii) Foresight-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (FC-RL) to refine the calibration and utility of the generated simulations. Evaluated on search and mathematical reasoning tasks, our approach consistently outperforms other training baselines. Our results demonstrate that effective internal world modeling in LLM agents requires a capability-first training pipeline to achieve grounded and calibrated foresight.

AI.tldr: They train LLM agents to explicitly imagine future states and predict plan success, achieving better long-horizon decision-making.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents are good at step-by-step decisions but stay reactive in long tasks; they don’t internally simulate “what-if” futures like humans or use a world model to assess plans, so their foresight is shallow and ungrounded when naively fine-tuned.
AI.method: They train a single autoregressive model that outputs both a verbal rollout of future states and a plan-conditioned success estimate (textual Q-value). To avoid mere imitation, they use a three-stage pipeline: (i) World Model Agentic Mid-Training (WM-AMT) to inject predictive world-model capabilities into the policy; (ii) Format-Eliciting Supervised Fine-Tuning (FE-SFT) to shape this capability into the desired textual rollout + value format; and (iii) Foresight-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (FC-RL) to calibrate and optimize the usefulness of the generated simulations for decision quality.
AI.result: On search tasks and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, their trained agents outperform standard training baselines that lack this structured internal world-modeling and foresight conditioning.
AI.conclusion: Grounded, calibrated foresight in LLM agents requires first building predictive capability and only then aligning it with explicit rollout/value formats and RL; this capability-first pipeline yields more effective internal world models and better performance on long-horizon tasks than naive look-ahead fine-tuning.

title: The Context-Ready Transformer
summary: We introduce the context-ready transformer, a new recurrent neural network architecture built from a D-layer transformer block that pre-contextualizes each token before it enters the block. During left-to-right generation, a correction network combines the previous position's block output -- a cached summary of past context -- with the current token embedding, so the tokenenters the block already contextualized rather than as a raw embedding. At sequential inference, the correction chain makes the architecture a recurrent neural network. For training, we unroll the correction process K times over the full sequence, processing all positions in parallel at each step. A pretrained transformer can also be converted to a context-ready model by adding a zero-initialized correction FFN and fine-tuning. We evaluate across widths, depths, block sizes, and two datasets, with all comparisons against standard transformers, variants, and ablations. A D=5 model beats a 12-layer transformer while generating 1.7x faster on an A100. With K=10, a single-layermodel (D=1) beats a 6-layer transformer with a 2.6x inference speedup, and sequential inference matches parallel K=10 to within 0.01 PPL. The architecture benefits most from wide representations and long contexts. On a pointer-chasing task, D=1 trained with BPTT solves all 10 composition levels, while standard transformers exhibit staircase-like depth dependence.

AI.tldr: They propose a recurrent-style transformer, the context-ready transformer, that pre-contextualizes tokens via a correction network, achieving better perplexity–speed tradeoffs than standard transformers.
AI.motivation: Standard transformers process raw token embeddings and rely on deep stacks for context integration, which can be inefficient for long contexts and sequential inference. The authors want a model that keeps transformer quality while gaining RNN-like recurrent computation and faster generation, especially in wide, long-context settings.
AI.method: They modify a D-layer transformer block by adding a correction network that, at each time step, combines the previous step’s block output (a cached summary of past context) with the current token embedding, so the input to the block is already contextualized. This makes the model recurrent at inference. For training, they unroll the correction process K times across the full sequence, allowing parallel processing at each unroll step. They also show how to convert a pretrained transformer by adding a zero-initialized correction feed-forward network and fine-tuning. They systematically vary depth D, unroll steps K, width, block size, and datasets, and compare with standard transformers, architectural variants, and ablations.
AI.result: A 5-layer context-ready model (D=5) outperforms a 12-layer standard transformer while generating 1.7× faster on an A100 GPU. With K=10, a single-layer model (D=1) surpasses a 6-layer transformer with a 2.6× inference speedup, and its sequential recurrent inference closely matches parallel K=10 training dynamics (within 0.01 perplexity). Performance gains are strongest for wide models and long-context tasks. On a synthetic pointer-chasing benchmark, the D=1 model trained with BPTT solves all 10 levels of compositional reasoning, whereas standard transformers show a staircase-like dependence on depth (failing at higher compositions unless sufficiently deep).
AI.conclusion: Context-ready transformers can turn a transformer block into an effective recurrent architecture that pre-contextualizes tokens, maintaining or improving language modeling quality while significantly accelerating sequential inference. The approach works even from pretrained transformers via a lightweight correction network and is especially advantageous for wide models and long-context and compositional reasoning tasks, where it outperforms depth-scaling in standard transformers.

title: Odyssey: Constructing Verifiable Local Truth-Preserving Foundation Models
summary: We introduce a categorical framework called ODYSSEY for constructing verifiable, local truth-preserving foundation models as compositions of foundries: building-block architectural components that specify a cover of local contexts, local representation families, restriction maps, gluing rules, obstruction policies, update obligations, and human-facing views. A foundry is an organized sheaf of knowledge that carries within it an argumentation component. Concrete foundries are built from generic foundries such as evidence/argument, operational decision, institutional/financial, market meaning, scientific challenge, research-program, assistant-build, and evaluation-harness foundries. Universal Foundry Learning (UFL) formalizes foundry construction as a composition of left and right Kan extensions, with left Kan extension rolling local artifacts into candidate foundries and right Kan extension enforcing the restriction, gluing, obstruction, and argumentation conditions required for promotion. Foundry SQL (FSQL) is a small typed query surface for slicing maintained foundry artifacts that uses TICKET (Topos Integration using Causal Kan Extension Transformers) certification for admitting external or pre-built models into durable ODYSSEY state. ODYSSEY is fully implemented and tested across a wide spectrum of concrete foundries, showing that the same categorical machinery supports domain construction, artifact replay, sheaf diagnostics, grounded Toulmin/local-LLM scrutiny, residual-obstruction ledgers, and optimized TICKET-compatible causal-claim extraction across heterogeneous sources. This paper is to be presented as a 2.5 hour tutorial at ICML 2026. The tutorial home page is at https://bit.ly/4ajS0nA.

AI.tldr: ODYSSEY is a categorical framework that builds verifiable, locally truth-preserving foundation models from modular “foundries” of structured knowledge, using sheaf-theoretic and Kan extension machinery, plus a query language (FSQL) and a certification mechanism (TICKET) to integrate, analyze, and verify heterogeneous AI systems.
AI.motivation: Current foundation models lack principled guarantees of local truthfulness, verifiability, and structured integration of heterogeneous domain knowledge, arguments, and decision artifacts. There is also a need for a unifying mathematical and systems framework that can (1) organize knowledge into local contexts, (2) support argumentation and obstruction tracking, (3) compose domain-specific reasoning modules, and (4) safely incorporate external models into a durable, auditable state. The paper proposes ODYSSEY to address these gaps, leveraging categorical and sheaf-theoretic tools to build modular, verifiable reasoning systems that can span different domains while preserving local correctness and providing human-interpretable views.
AI.method: The paper defines ODYSSEY as a categorical framework whose core modular elements are "foundries": organized sheaves of knowledge over local contexts. Each foundry specifies local representations, restriction maps, gluing rules, obstruction policies, update obligations, and human-facing views, and embeds an argumentation component. The authors define several generic foundry types (e.g., evidence/argument, operational decision, institutional/financial, market meaning, scientific challenge, research-program, assistant-build, evaluation-harness) and show how concrete foundries are constructed from these. Universal Foundry Learning (UFL) is introduced to formalize foundry construction as compositions of left and right Kan extensions: left Kan extensions aggregate local artifacts into candidate foundries, while right Kan extensions enforce restriction, gluing, obstruction, and argumentation constraints required for promotion to a valid foundry. Foundry SQL (FSQL), a small typed query language, allows slicing and querying foundry artifacts, while TICKET (Topos Integration using Causal Kan Extension Transformers) provides a certification mechanism for safely admitting external or pre-built models into ODYSSEY state. The framework is fully implemented and evaluated across multiple concrete foundries to demonstrate its generality.
AI.result: The implementation of ODYSSEY demonstrates that a single categorical machinery can support a diverse set of tasks: domain construction, artifact replay, sheaf diagnostics, grounded Toulmin-style argumentation and local-LLM scrutiny, maintenance of residual-obstruction ledgers, and optimized causal-claim extraction using TICKET across heterogeneous data sources and models. The experiments span a wide spectrum of concrete foundries, showing that the abstract foundry and UFL definitions are practically realizable and that FSQL and TICKET can be effectively used to query, manage, and certify complex, structured model artifacts and knowledge sheaves.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that ODYSSEY offers a unified, categorical framework for building verifiable, local truth-preserving foundation models as compositions of structured knowledge foundries. By leveraging sheaves, Kan extensions, and topos-theoretic ideas, it provides principled mechanisms for organizing domain knowledge, enforcing consistency and argumentation constraints, and safely integrating external models. The successful implementation across many concrete foundries suggests that this approach can systematically support trustworthy, auditable, and human-interpretable AI systems across heterogeneous domains. The work is mature enough to be delivered as an ICML tutorial, pointing to its dual role as both a research contribution and an educational introduction to categorical methods in AI systems design.

title: EntMTP: Accelerating LLM Inference with Entropy Guided Multi Token Prediction
summary: Multi-token prediction has been shown to increase data density during training, improve downstream text-generation quality, and serves as the defacto approach for self-speculative decoding. Existing foundation and open source models that use MTP heads commit to a static tree-based attention topology throughout the entire generation sequence, meaning the speculation depth, and thus the compute required during verification, stays constant regardless of the context. This is fundamentally misaligned with the entropy patterns of natural language where low-entropy regions often support reliable multi-step drafting, while high-entropy regions require more conservative speculation. To address this, we propose Entropy-guided Multi-Token Prediction (EntMTP), a training-free scheduler that toggles between tree-based attention topologies from a set of task-specific pareto-optimal trees conditioned on a running estimate of local generation entropy. By matching speculation depth to context predictability, EntMTP maximizes expected accepted-token throughput across the full distribution of generated text without sacrificing generation quality. When evaluated across Humaneval, ShareGPT, GSM8k, and Litbench benchmarks, EntMTP consistently achieves a 1.15x speedup against Hydra and peak speedup of 1.36x against Medusa baselines respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Entropy-guided Multi-Token Prediction (EntMTP), a training-free scheduler that dynamically adjusts multi-token speculation depth based on local entropy to speed up text generation without hurting quality.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-token prediction (MTP) systems use a fixed tree-based attention topology for the whole generation, so speculation depth and verification cost remain constant regardless of context. This is misaligned with natural language, where some segments are predictable (low entropy) and can be drafted many tokens ahead, while others are uncertain (high entropy) and require conservative speculation. The authors want to exploit this variability to increase decoding speed while preserving output quality.
AI.method: They propose Entropy-guided Multi-Token Prediction (EntMTP), which is a training-free scheduling algorithm layered on top of existing MTP models. EntMTP maintains a running estimate of local generation entropy and, depending on this estimate, switches among a set of task-specific Pareto-optimal tree-based attention topologies (i.e., different speculation depths and branching structures). In predictable regions, it selects deeper/more aggressive trees to speculate more tokens; in unpredictable regions, it chooses shallower trees to limit speculative depth and reduce wasted verification work.
AI.result: On several benchmarks—Humaneval, ShareGPT, GSM8k, and Litbench—EntMTP consistently accelerates decoding: about 1.15× speedup compared with Hydra and up to 1.36× peak speedup compared with Medusa, while maintaining comparable generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, entropy-aware control of multi-token speculation depth can better match compute to the predictability of the text being generated. By dynamically switching among Pareto-optimal tree topologies without retraining, EntMTP improves accepted-token throughput and end-to-end decoding speed relative to strong fixed-topology MTP baselines, without degrading output quality.

title: DysLexLens: A Low-Resource LLM Framework for Analysing Dyslexic Learners Insights from Online Forums
summary: Dyslexic learners increasingly use artificial intelligence (AI) tools to support reading, writing, organisation, and study-related tasks. However, their lived experiences with these tools remain largely underexamined. This paper proposes DysLexLens, a low-resource LLM framework, designed to analyse dyslexic learners experience with AI through online forum discussions. DysLexLens is designed as an end-to-end, evidence-traceable architecture which transforms noisy social media posts into a dictionary-driven corpora, provides knowledge-graph (KG)-based question reasoning, generates verifiable query responses, and enables response evaluation through quantitative and human-grounded assessment. DysLexLens has four key features. First, it employs a dictionary-driven filtering method to construct a more focused Reddit corpus on dyslexia and AI, filtering out noisy and weakly related posts to improve the relevance of data collected from low-resource forum contexts. Second, it integrates LLM-assisted semantic analysis with KG-based query reasoning to uncover meaningful patterns. Third, it has quantitative evaluation metrics (RAGAS and Query Robustness) to measure LLM-generated response performance. Fourth, it provides structured qualitative validation guidelines for assessing response quality, with a specific focus on hallucination and evidence alignment. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DysLexLens using dyslexia-related Reddit forum data and 30 questions. The results show its potential generalisability to other low-resource forum data contexts. DysLexLens, sample data, questions and evaluation results are available at Github to support reproducibility.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DysLexLens, a low-resource LLM framework to analyze dyslexic learners' experiences with AI tools using online forum data, focusing on evidence-traceable reasoning and multi-faceted evaluation.
AI.motivation: Despite increasing use of AI tools by dyslexic learners, there is limited understanding of their lived experiences as expressed in online forums, especially in low-resource, noisy data settings. Existing analyses lack robust, explainable pipelines to reliably extract, reason over, and evaluate insights from such data.
AI.method: The authors design DysLexLens, an end-to-end framework that: (1) filters and constructs a focused Reddit corpus on dyslexia and AI via a dictionary-driven approach; (2) combines LLM-assisted semantic analysis with knowledge-graph-based query reasoning; (3) evaluates LLM responses with quantitative metrics (RAGAS and Query Robustness); and (4) adds structured qualitative validation guidelines emphasizing hallucination detection and evidence alignment. They test it on dyslexia-related Reddit data and a set of 30 questions.
AI.result: Using Reddit posts related to dyslexia and AI, DysLexLens successfully builds a cleaner, more relevant corpus, supports KG-based reasoning over questions, and produces LLM responses that can be quantitatively scored and qualitatively checked. The reported experiments show good performance on RAGAS and robustness metrics and demonstrate that the framework scales to low-resource forum data and different query types.
AI.conclusion: DysLexLens provides an effective, evidence-traceable LLM framework for analyzing dyslexic learners' experiences with AI in low-resource online forums. Its modular pipeline (corpus construction, KG reasoning, quantitative and qualitative evaluation) is shown to work well on dyslexia-related Reddit data and is potentially generalizable to other domains with similarly noisy, sparse forum data. The public release of data, questions, and evaluation results supports reproducibility and future extensions.

title: Ko-WideSearch: A Korean Breadth-Search Benchmark for Exhaustive Set Enumeration by Web Agents
summary: Web-agent benchmarks overwhelmingly measure depth -- pinning one obscure answer behind a chain of constraints -- while breadth, exhaustively enumerating a closed set and filling each item's attributes, is barely evaluated, especially outside English. Breadth is also hard to build: certifying that a gold set is complete and every cell correct is far costlier than checking a single answer. I introduce \textsc{Ko-WideSearch}, a Korean breadth-search benchmark built by an automated synthesize-and-verify pipeline. Each task names a set-parent entity -- a TV season, a dynasty, a league, an administrative region, an election -- and asks for its full membership plus a per-item attribute table, graded by Item-, Column-, and Row-F1. It spans 228 tables over 190 entities and sixteen categories across three difficulty tiers, set by two structural knobs I dial independently -- table width and a 2-D composite key -- so cross-product membership climbs from 0\% to 100\% across the tiers. A single normalization-aware comparator is shared between gold construction and grading, so stable date and count columns are not over-dropped on formatting alone. Across twenty web agents, the failure is consistent: agents recover the set but not the rows (e.g.\ Item-F1 92.8 against Row-F1 53.7), accuracy falls steadily as the knobs harden, and neither more search nor more spend closes the gap. Broken down by cell, the hard part is finding the right value, not formatting it: open-ended free-text cells fail most, while cells with a standard answer such as a date or a name usually come out right.

AI.tldr: Introduces Ko-WideSearch, a Korean benchmark testing web agents’ ability to perform breadth-oriented table completion tasks, revealing a major gap between finding items and correctly filling their attributes.
AI.motivation: Most existing web-agent benchmarks focus on depth: retrieving a single obscure answer through complex constraints. They rarely test breadth tasks, which require exhaustively enumerating members of a set and filling in attributes for each, particularly in non-English contexts. Building such breadth benchmarks is difficult and expensive because it requires certifying completeness and correctness for entire tables, not just single answers.
AI.method: The author constructs Ko-WideSearch, a Korean breadth-search benchmark, using an automated synthesize-and-verify pipeline. Each task specifies a parent entity (e.g., TV season, dynasty, league, administrative region, election) and requires the agent to list all member items and complete an attribute table. Evaluation uses Item-, Column-, and Row-level F1 scores. The dataset includes 228 tables over 190 entities in 16 categories and three difficulty tiers. Difficulty is controlled via two independent structural parameters: table width and presence of a 2-D composite key, which together vary cross-product membership from 0% to 100%. A normalization-aware comparator, shared by both gold construction and grading, standardizes formats so that stable fields like dates and counts are not penalized for formatting differences.
AI.result: Across twenty tested web agents, there is a consistent pattern of failure: agents generally succeed at recovering the set of items (high Item-F1, e.g., 92.8) but perform poorly on completing full rows (much lower Row-F1, e.g., 53.7). Performance degrades as structural difficulty increases (wider tables and composite keys). Increasing search effort or spending does not substantially close this gap. Cell-level analysis shows that the main difficulty lies in retrieving the correct value, not in formatting: open-ended free-text cells have the highest error rates, while cells expecting standardized answers (dates, names) are usually correct.
AI.conclusion: Ko-WideSearch exposes a clear weakness in current web agents: they can identify set membership but struggle to accurately populate detailed attribute tables, especially under more complex structural conditions and for open-ended text fields. The benchmark, particularly in Korean, fills a gap in evaluation resources for breadth-oriented search and offers a controlled, format-robust framework for diagnosing and comparing web agents’ capabilities.

title: MER-R1: Multimodal Emotion Reasoning via Slow-Fast Thinking Synergy
summary: We find that explicit reasoning does not necessarily translate into better multimodal emotion recognition (MER) accuracy, even though it makes predictions more interpretable. Specifically, for reasoning-based MLLMs, fast thinking by triggering direct answers often outperforms slow thinking after deliberative reasoning. Our empirical analyses show that fast thinking improves recall with broader and more confident predictions, whereas slow thinking favors precision through conservative filtering of incorrect categories. Building on these insights, we propose MER-R1, a reinforcement learning framework that turns slow-fast complementarity into explicit optimization. Dual-objective disentanglement separates recall and precision into two optimization signals, allowing them to be jointly optimized rather than traded off against each other. Slow-fast confidence calibration further aligns the final slow-thinking answer with fast-thinking intuition, strengthening correct emotions while suppressing incorrect ones. In this way, MER-R1 unifies the recall-oriented intuition of fast thinking with the precision-oriented selectivity of slow thinking. We further provide theoretical justification for this synergy, showing that it mitigates variance-induced interference during optimization. Extensive experiments on MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion show that MER-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance and makes reasoning genuinely benefit emotion recognition.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that explicit step-by-step reasoning in multimodal large language models does not automatically improve multimodal emotion recognition (MER) accuracy, and proposes MER-R1, a reinforcement learning framework that combines fast (intuitive) and slow (deliberative) thinking to jointly optimize recall and precision, achieving state-of-the-art MER performance.
AI.motivation: Recent multimodal large language models can generate explicit reasoning chains, which improves interpretability, but it is unclear whether such reasoning actually improves task performance in multimodal emotion recognition. Empirically, the authors observe that direct, fast responses often outperform answers produced after long reasoning. They are motivated to understand this discrepancy, analyze the strengths of fast vs. slow thinking (recall vs. precision), and design a learning framework that turns their complementarity into real performance gains instead of a trade-off.
AI.method: The authors conduct empirical studies on reasoning-based MLLMs for MER, comparing fast thinking (direct answers) and slow thinking (deliberative reasoning before answering). They analyze how each mode affects recall and precision. Based on these insights, they propose MER-R1, a reinforcement learning framework with: (1) dual-objective disentanglement, which separates recall and precision into distinct optimization signals so both can be improved jointly; and (2) slow-fast confidence calibration, which aligns the final slow-thinking predictions with the fast-thinking intuition by amplifying correct emotions and suppressing incorrect ones suggested by fast thinking. They also provide a theoretical analysis showing that this joint optimization reduces variance-induced interference during training.
AI.result: Experiments on two benchmarks, MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion, demonstrate that MER-R1 achieves state-of-the-art performance on multimodal emotion recognition. The framework successfully turns explicit reasoning into an actual performance benefit, and empirical analyses confirm that fast thinking tends to enhance recall via broader, more confident predictions, while slow thinking enhances precision through conservative filtering of emotion categories.
AI.conclusion: Explicit reasoning alone does not guarantee better multimodal emotion recognition accuracy, because fast and slow thinking favor different error profiles (recall vs. precision). By explicitly modeling and optimizing this complementarity with MER-R1—using dual-objective disentanglement and slow-fast confidence calibration—one can jointly improve recall and precision and reduce optimization interference. MER-R1 thus makes reasoning genuinely useful for MER, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks and offering a principled way to harness fast and slow thinking in reasoning-based MLLMs.

title: Narrative-UFET: Narrative Generation for Ultra-Fine Entity Typing
summary: Ultra-fine entity typing (UFET) assigns highly specific types to entity mentions, but current approaches struggle with types in the long tail. We hypothesize that a key limitation is the reliance on sentence-level context, since disambiguating evidence is often spread across multiple sentences. Testing this has been difficult because all existing UFET resources are sentence-level. We present Narrative-UFET, a controlled extension of UFET in which each entity mention is paired with an automatically generated short, coherent narrative. Synthesizing narratives lets us isolate the effect of specific discourse properties. We experiment with two paired variants: one in which the entity's type is held constant across the narrative (Maintain) and one in which it shifts (Change). We show that narrative context yields consistent improvements on long-tail types over sentence-level baselines, with the Change variant providing the stronger signal. A comparison against naturally occurring contexts shows that synthetic narratives yield stronger gains, indicating that controlled discourse construction can surface signals that real text leaves implicit. Substantial room for improvement remains, suggesting open directions in both discourse modeling and narrative construction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Narrative-UFET, a dataset with synthetic short narratives around entities to study how multi-sentence discourse helps ultra-fine entity typing, particularly for rare long-tail types.
AI.motivation: Existing ultra-fine entity typing systems mainly use sentence-level context and perform poorly on rare, long-tail types. Disambiguating an entity’s fine-grained type often depends on information scattered across multiple sentences or turns in a narrative, but current datasets and setups do not let researchers isolate or systematically study these discourse-level effects.
AI.method: The authors automatically construct a controlled extension of an existing UFET resource by pairing each entity mention with a short, coherent synthetic narrative. They create two narrative variants: (1) Maintain, where the entity’s underlying type remains constant across the narrative, and (2) Change, where the entity’s type shifts over the narrative. These synthetic narratives are designed to manipulate discourse properties in a controlled way. They then train and evaluate UFET models using narrative-level context versus standard sentence-level baselines, and also compare synthetic narratives against naturally occurring multi-sentence contexts.
AI.result: Using narrative context improves performance on long-tail types compared with sentence-level baselines, with the Change narratives (where entity type changes) giving the strongest gains. When comparing synthetic narratives to real multi-sentence contexts, the synthetic, controlled narratives produce even larger improvements, suggesting that carefully constructed discourse can make type-disambiguating signals more explicit than in natural text.
AI.conclusion: Discourse-level narrative context measurably helps ultra-fine entity typing, especially for rare types, and synthetic, controlled narratives can reveal and amplify useful disambiguating cues beyond what is easily accessible in natural text. However, performance is still far from perfect, indicating significant open challenges in modeling discourse and in designing better narrative construction techniques.

title: ToE: A Hierarchical and Explainable Claim Verification Framework with Dynamic Multi-source Evidence Retrieval and Aggregation
summary: The rapid spread of fake news poses increasing threats to information ecosystems, especially as AI-generated misinformation under Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) poisoning allows adversarially crafted content to be systematically surfaced by retrieval systems, contaminating LLM reasoning. In this paper, we propose Tree of Evidence (ToE), a hierarchical evidence reasoning framework for automated fact-checking that models each claim as a dynamically expanding argument tree. ToE integrates a reinforcement learning-driven multi-source retrieval agent, an evidence evaluation agent, and an argument tree aggregation algorithm to iteratively decompose, retrieve, and verify claims through an explainable evidence chain. We further provide a theoretical analysis of the retrieval process, deriving a formal error bound that guarantees the learned policy converges to a neighborhood of the information-theoretically optimal policy. Experiments across multiple datasets and backbone LLMs demonstrate that ToE achieves improvements ranging from 4 to 24 percentage points over competitive baselines, with particularly pronounced gains on adversarially poisoned inputs.

AI.tldr: Tree of Evidence (ToE) is a hierarchical, explainable fact-checking framework that builds argument trees using multi-source retrieval and reinforcement learning to robustly verify claims, even under adversarial GEO poisoning.
AI.motivation: Fake news and AI-generated misinformation, especially via Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) poisoning, can be systematically surfaced by retrieval systems and corrupt LLM reasoning. Existing fact-checking approaches struggle with robustness, multi-step reasoning, and explainability under such adversarial conditions. The paper aims to create a more resilient, theoretically grounded, and interpretable fact-checking system.
AI.method: The authors propose Tree of Evidence (ToE), which models each claim as a dynamically expanding argument tree. The framework combines: (1) a reinforcement learning-driven multi-source retrieval agent that learns how and where to retrieve evidence; (2) an evidence evaluation agent that judges the relevance and veracity of retrieved information; and (3) an argument tree aggregation algorithm that incrementally decomposes the claim, organizes evidence, and builds a structured reasoning chain. They also provide a theoretical analysis of the retrieval process, proving an error bound showing the RL policy converges near an information-theoretically optimal retrieval policy.
AI.result: Across multiple datasets and different backbone LLMs, ToE outperforms strong fact-checking baselines by 4–24 percentage points in accuracy or related metrics. The gains are especially large on inputs that have been adversarially poisoned via GEO, indicating improved robustness to AI-generated misinformation and retrieval pollution.
AI.conclusion: ToE offers a robust, explainable, and theoretically grounded solution for automated fact-checking in environments polluted by AI-generated misinformation. By structuring claims as argument trees and optimizing retrieval via reinforcement learning, it significantly boosts performance—particularly under adversarial GEO poisoning—suggesting a promising path toward more reliable fact-checking and LLM reasoning in contaminated information ecosystems.

title: Masked Language Flow Models
summary: Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) promise fast, parallel language generation, but their reverse transition factorises across token positions -- an approximation that breaks down in the few-step sampling regime where parallel generation ought to provide the greatest efficiency gains. Flow Language Models (FLMs) sidestep this limitation by learning a continuous flow that transports noise toward clean sequences represented in Euclidean space, inducing a flow map that can be distilled for single-step generation. However, this makes complex tasks requiring multi-step reasoning problematic for FLMs, as FLMs are forced to decode every token during generation. To address this, we introduce Masked Language Flow Models (MLFMs), which incorporate masking into FLMs using a continuous stochastic interpolant to bridge partially masked and clean sequences. This design enables conditional generation via continuous flows and allows pretrained MDMs to be converted into MLFMs through a simple, lightweight adaptation. Leveraging this flexibility, we propose a novel sampler that alternates continuous denoising with the discrete unmasking of confident tokens to better support multi-step reasoning. We evaluate our approach on GSM8K and MT-Bench and find, for the first time, that flow-based language models can be scaled to solve downstream reasoning and instruction-following tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Masked Language Flow Models (MLFMs), which combine strengths of masked diffusion models and flow language models to enable efficient, multi-step reasoning and instruction-following with flow-based generators.
AI.motivation: Masked Diffusion Models allow fast parallel language generation but rely on an independence assumption across token positions that fails when using few denoising steps, precisely where speedups should be largest. Flow Language Models avoid this by learning continuous flows in Euclidean sequence space and can be distilled to single-step generators, but they must decode all tokens every step, making them ill-suited for complex multi-step reasoning tasks that benefit from partial updates and iterative refinement. There is thus a need for a generative framework that preserves the continuous flow advantages of FLMs, while gaining the conditional, partially-masked generation capabilities of MDMs to better support reasoning-heavy language tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose Masked Language Flow Models (MLFMs), which integrate masking into flow-based language modeling. They construct a continuous stochastic interpolant that smoothly connects partially masked sequences with fully clean sequences in Euclidean space. This setup enables conditional generation via continuous flows conditioned on which tokens are masked or unmasked. They also demonstrate a lightweight adaptation procedure that converts pretrained Masked Diffusion Models into MLFMs. On top of this architecture, they design a new sampling algorithm that alternates between continuous denoising steps in the flow space and discrete unmasking of tokens whose predictions are sufficiently confident, thereby gradually revealing the final sequence in a way that supports multi-step reasoning.
AI.result: MLFMs are empirically evaluated on GSM8K, a math word problem benchmark, and MT-Bench, an instruction-following and dialogue evaluation benchmark. The experiments show that, unlike prior flow-based language models, MLFMs can be scaled to perform competitively on downstream reasoning and instruction-following tasks. The proposed alternating denoising–unmasking sampler leads to better support for multi-step reasoning compared to naive single-step or fully parallel decoding approaches. Overall, the results demonstrate that integrating masking and continuous flows over sequences enables flow-based models to handle more complex language generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: By introducing Masked Language Flow Models, the paper unifies advantages of masked diffusion and flow-based language modeling: efficient, parallelizable continuous flows with the ability to perform conditional, partially-masked generation. The continuous stochastic interpolant between masked and clean sequences, together with the alternating denoising–unmasking sampler, overcomes key limitations of both MDMs and FLMs in the low-step, reasoning-intensive regime. The success on GSM8K and MT-Bench provides initial evidence that flow-based language models, when augmented with masking, can be viable architectures for scalable reasoning and instruction-following in natural language generation.

title: Towards Reliable and Robust LLM Planning: Symbolic Feedback-Driven Iterative Self-Refinement Framework
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have attracted widespread attention from academia and industry, yet their deployment raises critical security concerns regarding robustness and reliability. Planning, a core component of intelligent behavior, remains challenging for LLMs, which often produce infeasible or incorrect solutions in long-horizon decision-making tasks due to inherent complexity. In this paper, we propose a symbolic feedback-driven iterative self-refinement framework to enhance the robustness and reliability of LLMs in long-horizon planning. Specifically, a natural language prompting mechanism is introduced to map logical symbols into natural language descriptions, enabling LLMs to better capture task constraints and semantics. We further design a symbolic verifier that identifies errors and converts them into corrective instructions interpretable by the LLM, thereby guiding self-refinement. In addition, we leverage a plan recognizer to infer goal reachability, facilitating more effective guidance toward desired goals. Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently improves both feasibility and correctness in long-horizon planning tasks. This highlights its effectiveness in enhancing the reliability of LLM-based planning and potential to enable more trustworthy AI systems.

AI.tldr: They propose a symbolic feedback-driven iterative self-refinement framework that uses natural language mappings of symbols, a symbolic verifier, and a plan recognizer to improve LLM robustness and reliability on long-horizon planning tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used but struggle with robustness and reliability, especially in long-horizon planning where they often generate infeasible or incorrect plans due to task complexity. There is a need to make LLM-based planning more robust, reliable, and trustworthy.
AI.method: The authors introduce a symbolic feedback-driven iterative self-refinement framework. They (1) design a natural language prompting mechanism that maps logical symbols to natural language so LLMs can better understand constraints and task semantics; (2) develop a symbolic verifier that checks generated plans, detects errors, and turns them into corrective instructions that the LLM can interpret to iteratively refine its plan; and (3) incorporate a plan recognizer to infer whether goals are reachable, thus guiding the refinement process toward achievable goals.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on long-horizon planning tasks show that the framework consistently improves both feasibility (plans satisfy constraints and are executable) and correctness (plans actually achieve the intended goals) compared to baseline LLM planning without such symbolic feedback and refinement.
AI.conclusion: Integrating symbolic feedback, verification, and plan recognition into an iterative self-refinement loop significantly enhances the robustness and reliability of LLMs in long-horizon planning, suggesting a promising direction for building more trustworthy LLM-based AI planning systems.

title: Cross-Platform Chinese Offensive Comment Detection via Dual-Threshold Hard Example Mining
summary: Cross-platform deployment of offensive comment detection for Chinese social media suffers performance degradation. The paper proposes a dual-threshold hard mining method to address this. First, the clean-Chinese-base RoBERTa is finetuned on COLD to establish a binary baseline for fair comparison. Second, a three-class fine-labeled test set covering Weibo, Xiaohongshu, Tieba, and Zhihu is constructed, domain distances from the source are quantified using Jaccard and Proxy-A Distance, as well as the degradation bottleneck of the baseline under domain shift is systematically revealed. Herein, a dual threshold hard example mining strategy is proposed. High- and low-confidence error-prone samples are filtered from unlabeled corpora by prediction confidence. The model is secondarily finetuned under implicit contexts with merely a small set of manually labeled hard examples, realizing low-cost cross-platform domain adaptation. Experiments reveal significant performance gains of the optimized model across four platforms.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles performance drops when deploying Chinese offensive comment detection models across different social media platforms, and proposes a dual-threshold hard example mining strategy for low-cost cross-platform domain adaptation, achieving significant performance gains.
AI.motivation: Chinese offensive comment detection models trained on one platform (source domain) perform poorly when deployed on other platforms (target domains) such as Weibo, Xiaohongshu, Tieba, and Zhihu due to domain shift (different language styles, vocabularies, and contexts). Existing cross-platform solutions can be expensive, requiring large amounts of labeled data or complex adaptation schemes. There is a need for a more cost-effective way to adapt models across platforms while maintaining or improving performance.
AI.method: 1) Start with a clean-Chinese-base RoBERTa model and finetune it on the COLD dataset to build a binary offensive/non-offensive baseline. 2) Construct a fine-labeled three-class test set that spans four Chinese social media platforms (Weibo, Xiaohongshu, Tieba, Zhihu) and measure domain distances from the source domain using Jaccard distance and Proxy-A Distance to quantify domain shift. 3) Analyze how the baseline model degrades under domain shift to identify bottlenecks. 4) Propose a dual-threshold hard example mining strategy: use model prediction confidence on unlabeled target-platform corpora to select both high-confidence and low-confidence error-prone samples (hard examples). 5) Manually annotate only a small subset of these hard examples and conduct a second finetuning of the model under these implicit contextual signals to realize domain adaptation with limited labeling effort.
AI.result: The adapted model, trained with the dual-threshold hard example mining approach and a small labeled set of hard samples from target platforms, shows substantial performance improvements over the baseline across all four evaluated social media platforms. The experiments validate that selectively mining and labeling confidence-based hard examples provides effective cross-platform domain adaptation with reduced annotation cost, overcoming a large part of the degradation observed under domain shift.
AI.conclusion: Dual-threshold hard example mining is an effective and low-cost strategy for cross-platform deployment of Chinese offensive comment detection models. By quantifying domain differences, exposing the weaknesses of a baseline model under domain shift, and then focusing limited labeling resources on confidence-based hard samples, the method significantly boosts performance across multiple social media platforms. This demonstrates a practical pathway for scalable, real-world offensive language detection in heterogeneous Chinese online environments.

title: Understanding Rollout Error in Graph World Models
summary: World models are often used for planning by rolling learned dynamics forward. Many planning environments, however, are not vectors or images; they are graphs of agents, tools, skills, routes, and dependencies. In these settings, a local prediction error may stay local or spread through the graph, and the failure mode changes again when edges are predicted rather than fixed. This paper studies long-horizon rollout error in Graph World Models (GWMs). We formulate a unified fixed-edge and dynamic-edge GWM framework with action nodes for node-, edge-, and graph-level decisions. We develop graph-valued rollout bounds that separate topology-induced amplification from model-induced amplification, and we introduce a joint node-edge operator for dynamic-edge rollouts. Guided by the analysis, we propose Error-Aware GWM, which combines spectral regularization, rollout consistency, and critical-node weighting. Across synthetic topologies and heterogeneous agent-graph testbeds, rollout error and planning regret grow with horizon, dynamic-edge training is needed when structure evolves, and Error-Aware GWM prevents long-horizon divergence while preserving prediction accuracy. Real-world graph benchmarks clarify the scope of GWMs: they are most useful for dynamic graph rollout and agent planning, while specialized graph models remain strong on static or sparse prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes and improves world models defined on graphs—Graph World Models (GWMs)—by characterizing how rollout errors grow over long horizons and proposing an error-aware training method that stabilizes long-horizon planning on dynamic graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing world models typically assume vector or image states, but many planning problems are naturally graphs (agents, tools, routes, dependencies). In such graph environments, prediction errors can propagate along the graph structure in complex ways, especially when edges are themselves predicted and can change over time. There is a lack of theoretical understanding and practical methods to control long-horizon rollout error and planning regret in these graph-based settings.
AI.method: 1) Formulate a unified framework for Graph World Models that covers both fixed-edge and dynamic-edge settings, with explicit action nodes supporting node-, edge-, and graph-level decisions. 2) Derive graph-valued bounds on rollout error that decompose error amplification into two parts: one due to graph topology and one due to imperfections in the learned model; introduce a joint node–edge operator to model dynamic-edge rollouts. 3) Based on this analysis, design Error-Aware GWM, which incorporates (a) spectral regularization to control topology-induced amplification, (b) rollout-consistency training to align multi-step predictions with single-step dynamics, and (c) critical-node weighting to focus learning on nodes that most affect long-horizon performance. 4) Empirically evaluate on synthetic graph topologies, heterogeneous agent-graph environments, and real-world graph benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical studies show that (i) rollout error and planning regret increase with the planning horizon; (ii) when the graph structure evolves over time, training with dynamic edges is necessary for good performance; (iii) the proposed Error-Aware GWM significantly reduces long-horizon divergence while maintaining or improving prediction accuracy compared to baselines. On real-world graph benchmarks, GWMs perform particularly well for tasks involving dynamic graph rollout and agent planning, while more specialized graph models still excel on static or sparse prediction tasks.
AI.conclusion: Graph World Models are a suitable and powerful framework for planning in graph-structured environments, especially when both nodes and edges evolve over time. By analyzing how errors propagate along graph topology and through the learned dynamics, and by incorporating this understanding into training via Error-Aware GWM, it is possible to mitigate long-horizon rollout error and planning regret without sacrificing accuracy.
