GWMs are thus best applied to dynamic, planning-intensive graph problems, whereas traditional graph prediction models remain preferable for static or sparsely changing graphs.

title: Yuvion LLM: An Adversarially-Aware Large Language Model for Content And AI Safety
summary: As large language models are increasingly deployed in real-world systems, safety failures can still lead to harmful outputs and dangerous misuse. We argue that the essence of safety is adversarial: many failures arise not from natural inputs alone, but from strategic attempts to evade model policies and safeguards. However, existing general-purpose model development largely overlook this adversarial nature, and often remain insufficient for realistic safety scenarios involving planning, tool use, and multi-step reasoning, causing measured safety performance to overestimate real deployment robustness. To address this gap, we present Yuvion LLM, a large language model built for adversarially robust content safety and broader AI safety. Yuvion LLM treats adversarial robustness and agentic capability as first-class objectives. Its pipeline combines adversarially aware data construction, knowledge-enhanced continued pretraining, and policy-grounded multi-task safety post-training, including risk-aware supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning-based policy optimization, together with safety-aware agentic reinforcement learning for tool use and multi-step reasoning in complex safety scenarios. We further introduce the Yuvion LLM RiskEval (YLRE), a collection of 93 benchmarks across four evaluation categories, covering diverse open and internal evaluations with a focus on safety, adversarial robustness, and real-world capability requirements. Across these evaluations, Yuvion LLM demonstrates clear advantages on safety-focused benchmarks and particularly strong robustness under adversarial conditions, while maintaining solid overall capability. Notably, Yuvion-8B outperforms most state-of-the-art baselines, including substantially larger models such as GPT-5.4 and Qwen3-MAX, on several safety tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Yuvion LLM, a large language model specifically designed for adversarially robust safety, along with a comprehensive safety and robustness benchmark suite called YLRE.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety work often evaluates on relatively clean, non-adversarial inputs and does not fully capture challenging real-world settings involving strategic attempts to bypass safeguards, multi-step reasoning, planning, and tool use. This leads to an overestimation of how safe and robust models actually are in deployment. The authors aim to build an LLM and evaluation framework that treat adversarial robustness and agentic behavior as primary design goals, rather than afterthoughts.
AI.method: The authors design Yuvion LLM with a safety-centric training pipeline: (1) adversarially aware data construction that includes strategically crafted safety-relevant and adversarial examples; (2) knowledge-enhanced continued pretraining to strengthen domain knowledge important for safety; (3) policy-grounded multi-task safety post-training, which combines risk-aware supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning–based policy optimization on safety objectives; and (4) safety-aware agentic reinforcement learning to handle tool use and multi-step reasoning while respecting safety constraints. Additionally, they create Yuvion LLM RiskEval (YLRE), a suite of 93 benchmarks in four categories targeting safety, adversarial robustness, and practical capability requirements.
AI.result: On the YLRE suite and other internal and open benchmarks, Yuvion LLM achieves higher scores than competing models on safety-focused and adversarial robustness tasks, while still performing competitively on general capabilities. In particular, the 8-billion-parameter Yuvion-8B model surpasses several state-of-the-art baselines, including larger models such as GPT-5.4 and Qwen3-MAX, on multiple safety evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Treating adversarial robustness and agentic capability as first-class design objectives, combined with a specialized safety-centric training pipeline and a comprehensive evaluation suite, leads to LLMs that are more robust under adversarial conditions without sacrificing overall utility. Yuvion LLM and the YLRE benchmarks demonstrate a viable path toward safer, more deployment-ready language models in adversarial real-world environments.

title: Grounded Iterative Language Planning: How Parameterized World Models Reduce Hallucination Propagation in LLM Agents
summary: World models for language agents come in two useful forms. An agent-based world model calls an LLM API and reasons flexibly in language, but its errors appear as hallucinated state changes that are hard to score with ordinary regression losses. A parameterized world model is a trained transition predictor; its errors are easier to measure with quantities such as NodeMSE, delta accuracy, and validity accuracy, but it is usually weaker as a standalone planner. We compare these two families on four graph-structured planning benchmarks and introduce operational hallucination metrics for the agent-based case. The comparison motivates \textbf{Grounded Iterative Language Planning} (GILP), which trains only a small parameterized backbone and combines it with API-based agent reasoning. The backbone supplies valid actions, predicted state deltas, risk, and value; the LLM drafts an action and imagined delta; and a consistency gate asks for revision when the two disagree. On real GPT-4o-mini calls, GILP reduces hallucinated-state rate from 0.176 to 0.035. In calibrated simulator ablations, it raises success from 0.668 to 0.838 while adding only ~22% extra LLM calls.

AI.tldr: The paper compares two types of world models for language-based planning and proposes GILP, a hybrid approach that uses a small learned backbone plus an LLM with a consistency gate to reduce hallucinated state changes and improve planning success with modest extra LLM calls.
AI.motivation: Language-agent world models that rely only on LLM APIs can reason flexibly but often hallucinate incorrect state transitions that are hard to evaluate and control. Purely parameterized transition models are easy to train and score but are weaker planners. There is a need to combine the strengths of both, especially on graph-structured planning tasks, and to quantitatively measure and reduce operational hallucinations in agent-based models.
AI.method: The authors (1) define and compare two families of world models—agent-based (LLM-API planners) and parameterized (trained transition predictors)—on four graph-structured planning benchmarks; (2) introduce new operational hallucination metrics for the agent-based models; and (3) propose Grounded Iterative Language Planning (GILP), which trains a compact parameterized backbone that predicts valid actions, state deltas, risk, and value, and pairs it with an LLM that proposes actions and imagined deltas. A consistency gate checks agreement between the LLM and backbone, triggering revision when they disagree. They evaluate using real GPT-4o-mini calls and simulator-based ablations.
AI.result: On real GPT-4o-mini experiments, GILP cuts the hallucinated-state rate from 0.176 to 0.035. In calibrated simulator ablations, it increases task success rate from 0.668 to 0.838 while requiring only about 22% more LLM calls compared to the baseline. These results show that the hybrid design significantly improves reliability and performance without large additional LLM cost.
AI.conclusion: A small, trained transition backbone combined with an LLM planner and a consistency gate yields a more grounded language agent that both hallucinates less and solves more planning tasks than purely agent-based or purely parameterized world models. Carefully designed hybrid systems and explicit operational hallucination metrics are effective for improving world modeling and planning in graph-structured environments.

title: When Search Agents Should Ask: DiscoBench for Clarification-Aware Deep Search
summary: Search agents powered by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to solve complex information-seeking tasks, requiring multi-step retrieval and reasoning to fulfill user goals. However, existing benchmarks often assume that user queries are complete and explicit, overlooking the fact that real-world search requests are frequently vague, underspecified, or even factually incorrect. In deep search scenarios, such ambiguity can propagate along multi-step reasoning chains and lead agents toward incorrect search trajectories. To address this gap, we introduce DiscoBench, a benchmark for clarification-aware deep search, designed to evaluate whether search agents can proactively identify ambiguity, ask effective clarification questions, and recover correct reasoning paths through user interaction. DiscoBench contains 211 samples and 463 ambiguity instances across 11 real-world domains, covering four ambiguity types. We further design a user simulator for multi-turn interaction and evaluate model performance from four perspectives: task utility, ambiguity detection, interaction strategy, and cost efficiency. Experiments on representative LLMs show that ambiguity detection and effective clarification are distinct capabilities, and that repeatedly searching instead of asking for clarification often performs worse than direct guessing, highlighting a critical gap between retrieval ability and interactive problem-solving in current search agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiscoBench, a benchmark to evaluate how well LLM-based search agents detect and clarify ambiguity in complex, multi-step search tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-world user search queries are often vague, incomplete, or partially wrong, but most existing benchmarks assume clean, fully specified queries. In deep, multi-step search, such ambiguities can mislead agents and compound errors along the reasoning chain. There is a lack of systematic evaluation of whether LLM-based search agents can notice these issues, ask for clarification, and recover correct search trajectories.
AI.method: The authors build DiscoBench, a clarification-aware deep search benchmark with 211 samples and 463 ambiguity instances over 11 real-world domains. They categorize four types of ambiguity, embed them in multi-step search scenarios, and design a user simulator that can engage in multi-turn interactions with search agents. They then evaluate various LLM-based agents along four dimensions: task utility (solving the task), ambiguity detection (spotting unclear or incorrect queries), interaction strategy (how and when they ask clarification questions), and cost efficiency (resource usage).
AI.result: Experiments with representative LLMs show that current search agents struggle: detecting ambiguity and asking good clarification questions turn out to be separate, non-trivially correlated skills. Agents that simply keep searching without asking the user for clarification often perform worse than agents that just guess directly, indicating that naive repeated retrieval doesn’t fix ambiguity-induced errors.
AI.conclusion: DiscoBench exposes a significant gap between current LLMs’ retrieval capabilities and their interactive problem-solving skills in realistic, ambiguous deep search settings. Effective search agents must be able not only to retrieve information but also to recognize underspecified or incorrect user queries and engage in clarification dialogue to maintain correct reasoning paths.

title: ATOD: Annealed Turn-aware On-policy Distillation for Multi-turn Autonomous Agents
summary: Training small language-model agents for long-horizon interactive tasks requires both fast imitation and reward-driven improvement. On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense teacher guidance and typically improves rapidly in the early stage, but its gains saturate once the student approaches the teacher, limiting the final performance ceiling. Reinforcement learning (RL) directly optimizes environment rewards and encourages exploratory improvement toward a higher reward-defined ceiling, but sparse and delayed feedback makes early-stage learning much less efficient than OPD. In this paper, we propose ATOD (Annealed Turn-aware On-policy Distillation), a hybrid online distillation algorithm that explicitly exploits this complementarity. (1) ATOD uses an annealed OPD-RL schedule: OPD dominates early training to approach teacher-level behavior, while RL is gradually strengthened to drive reward-based exploration. (2) ATOD introduces Turn-level Disagreement-Uncertainty Reweighting (T-DUR), which softly amplifies high-utility turns and improves dense supervision in long trajectories. Experiments on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA show that ATOD consistently outperforms competing post-training baselines: across the three student sizes, ATOD improves average success rate by 3.03 points over OPD and 23.62 points over GRPO, while surpassing the corresponding teacher models by 2.16 points.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ATOD, a hybrid algorithm that combines on-policy distillation and reinforcement learning with an annealed schedule and a turn-level reweighting mechanism to train small language-model agents more efficiently and to higher final performance on long-horizon interactive tasks.
AI.motivation: Small language-model agents need to learn long-horizon interactive tasks efficiently and to a high final performance. On-policy distillation (OPD) learns quickly early on by imitating a teacher but plateaus at the teacher’s level, while reinforcement learning (RL) can surpass the teacher via reward-driven exploration but learns slowly early due to sparse, delayed rewards. The authors are motivated to design a training approach that leverages the fast, dense supervision of OPD and the higher performance ceiling of RL, while also addressing the difficulty of providing informative supervision over long trajectories.
AI.method: The authors propose ATOD (Annealed Turn-aware On-policy Distillation), a hybrid online distillation algorithm. First, it uses an annealed schedule that balances OPD and RL: training starts with OPD dominating to quickly match teacher behavior, then gradually increases the weight of RL to drive reward-based exploration and improvement beyond the teacher. Second, they introduce Turn-level Disagreement-Uncertainty Reweighting (T-DUR), a mechanism that identifies turns in an interaction trajectory where the student and teacher disagree or where there is higher uncertainty, and softly upweights these turns to provide more informative, dense supervision on high-utility steps. This makes better use of the teacher signal in long-horizon trajectories while still incorporating RL reward signals.
AI.result: In experiments on three long-horizon interactive benchmarks—ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA—ATOD is compared against other post-training baselines, including standard OPD and a reinforcement-learning method, GRPO. Across three different student model sizes, ATOD improves average success rate by 3.03 percentage points over OPD and by 23.62 percentage points over GRPO, showing both better sample efficiency and higher final performance. Moreover, ATOD-trained students surpass their teacher models on average by 2.16 percentage points in success rate, demonstrating that the hybrid OPD+RL approach can exceed the teacher’s performance ceiling.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that combining on-policy distillation with reinforcement learning via an annealed training schedule, together with turn-level reweighting of high-utility supervision signals, yields more effective training for small language-model agents on long-horizon interactive tasks. ATOD leverages the complementary strengths of OPD (fast early learning) and RL (exploratory improvement beyond the teacher) and provides denser, better-targeted supervision in long trajectories. The empirical results across multiple benchmarks and student sizes indicate that ATOD offers a practical and generally stronger post-training method that can not only close the gap to but also surpass teacher performance.

title: From Signals to Transfer: A Factorised Study of Probe-Based Uncertainty Estimation in Large Language Models
summary: Probe-based uncertainty estimation (UE) has emerged as a prominent approach to detect hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) by learning uncertainty from internal model signals. Yet, recent methods vary simultaneously across feature design, training data construction, and evaluation setting, obscuring what actually drives performance. To address this issue, we propose a factorised study of probe-based UE under matched conditions. Our results show that raw hidden states and attention features are difficult to outperform in-domain. However, under distribution shift, structured and compressed features are more robust, suggesting that in-domain performance alone is insufficient to measure progress. Furthermore, prompting and label construction significantly affect probe behaviour. Building on these best-practice findings, we train benchmark-based pretrained probes that transfer reasonably well to open-ended factual generation, providing a stable off-the-shelf baseline. Our work encourages more deployment-oriented evaluation of probe-based uncertainty estimators. The code repository is available at https://github.com/ponhvoan/ProbeUE.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically studies probe-based uncertainty estimation for detecting hallucinations in LLMs, disentangling the effects of feature design, training data, and evaluation setup, and provides best-practice insights plus pretrained probes as baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing probe-based uncertainty estimation methods for hallucination detection differ simultaneously in many aspects (features, data, evaluation), making it unclear which design choices truly improve performance and how robust they are, especially under distribution shift. A controlled, factorised comparison is needed to guide method development and deployment.
AI.method: The authors perform a factorised empirical study of probe-based uncertainty estimators under matched conditions. They compare different internal feature types (raw hidden states, attention, structured/compressed representations), investigate the impact of prompting and label construction, and evaluate both in-domain and under distribution shift. Based on what works best, they train probes on benchmark datasets to create pretrained, reusable uncertainty estimators.
AI.result: In-domain, probes using raw hidden states or attention features are hard to outperform. Under distribution shift, however, more structured and compressed features show better robustness, indicating that focusing only on in-domain scores is misleading. They also find that prompting strategy and the way supervision labels are constructed strongly influence probe behavior. Pretrained benchmark-based probes exhibit reasonable transfer performance to open-ended factual generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Progress in probe-based uncertainty estimation cannot be judged by in-domain performance alone; robustness under distribution shift and sensitivity to design choices like feature representation, prompting, and label construction are crucial. Structured/compressed features, carefully chosen prompts, and standardized label creation improve robustness, and the provided pretrained probes offer a strong, deployment-oriented baseline for future work on hallucination detection in LLMs.

title: NormAct: A Benchmark for Hidden Social Norm Compliance in Embodied Planning
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly deployed as embodied planners in egocentric environments, where task success requires not only achieving instructed goals but also acting in socially appropriate ways. While explicit goals may render certain actions optimal, implicit social norms often impose hidden constraints. Existing evaluations typically focus on explicit goal achievement or direct norm knowledge, seldom assessing whether planners can infer and apply these hidden constraints within action sequences. We introduce NormAct, a benchmark for embodied social-norm interactions that evaluates plans on Goal Achievement, Norm Compliance, and overall Task Success. NormAct uniquely embeds hidden norms within ordinary tasks, testing whether models can realize them without explicit instruction. Experiments with state-of-the-art MLLMs (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3 Pro) reveal a significant gap: models achieve explicit goals in 67.3\% of cases, but comply with hidden norms in only 26.4\%. Cue-condition experiments indicate that this gap stems not from a lack of general social knowledge, but from challenges in activating and grounding relevant norms in context. To address this, we propose NormPerceptor, a context-conditioned cue generator that infers scene-relevant norms prior to planning, increasing Task Success from 24.2\% to 46.7\%. Our results underscore the importance of enabling embodied agents to proactively detect hidden norms, ground them in visual evidence, and integrate them as action-planning constraints. Our benchmark is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Caleb196x/NormAct.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NormAct, a benchmark to test whether multimodal LLM-based embodied planners can follow hidden social norms while achieving task goals, and proposes NormPerceptor, a cue-generation module that substantially improves norm-compliant task success.
AI.motivation: As multimodal LLMs are increasingly used as embodied agents in egocentric environments, they must not only complete explicit tasks but also behave in socially acceptable ways. Existing evaluations mainly check goal completion or direct norm recall, not whether agents can infer and apply implicit social norms that function as hidden constraints on action. There is a need for systematic evaluation and methods that help agents detect and use such norms in planning.
AI.method: The authors build NormAct, a benchmark of egocentric embodied tasks where each task has explicit goals plus embedded hidden social norms that are not explicitly stated. They define three metrics: Goal Achievement, Norm Compliance, and overall Task Success (which requires both). They evaluate several state-of-the-art MLLMs (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini 3 Pro) on this benchmark to analyze their ability to detect and respect hidden norms. They also design NormPerceptor, a context-conditioned cue generator that, given the scene, infers likely relevant social norms before the planning step and passes these norm cues to the planner as constraints.
AI.result: On NormAct, tested MLLMs achieve explicit goals in 67.3% of cases but comply with hidden norms in only 26.4%, yielding low Task Success. Additional experiments show that models do possess general social knowledge but fail to activate and ground the right norms in specific contexts. Incorporating NormPerceptor significantly improves performance, increasing overall Task Success from 24.2% to 46.7% by generating context-specific norm cues that guide planning.
AI.conclusion: MLLM-based embodied planners currently struggle to recognize and apply implicit social norms during action planning, even though they know these norms in the abstract. Hidden norms must be proactively detected, grounded in visual context, and integrated as planning constraints. The NormAct benchmark exposes this gap and provides a testbed, while NormPerceptor shows that adding a norm-perception stage can substantially improve norm-compliant task performance, pointing toward future work on socially-aware embodied agents.

title: Mitigating LLM-based p-Hacking by Preregistering for the Next LLM
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate, classify, and annotate data whose outputs feed downstream hypothesis tests. However, LLM-based research is easy to p-hack: a researcher can tune the prompts, decoding parameters, or output format until a desired result is reached. We propose a protocol to mitigate p-hacking in LLM-based research: preregistering the experiment and eligible models, and then running it on the first eligible LLM that is released after the preregistration. The researcher finalizes the procedure on current models, preregisters the analysis plan together with a set of eligible future models, and runs the confirmatory analysis on the first eligible model released afterward. Because this model does not exist at commitment time, it cannot be hacked against; furthermore, configurations that hack one model frequently do not transfer to the next. We evaluate the protocol on two tasks whose true values are known. Across 20 models from four providers and 11 LLM-analysis configurations, the protocol would have blocked successful transfer of the p-hack in 73.9% and 72.7% of cases in the two tasks. Additional analyses reveal that mitigation remains substantial under several stress tests. Finally, putting money where our mouth is, we followed our own protocol and preregistered our experiment. The preregistered experiment confirmed the protocol's effectiveness: out of the 7 configurations that hacked the prior model, the hacking failed to carry over in 6 configurations on the first eligible model released afterward.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and empirically validates a protocol to prevent p-hacking in research that relies on large language models by preregistering analyses and committing in advance to use the first eligible future model that is released.
AI.motivation: Research using LLMs is vulnerable to p-hacking because researchers can freely tweak prompts, decoding settings, and output formats until they obtain statistically significant or desired results, undermining the credibility and reproducibility of findings. Existing statistical safeguards don’t directly address the unique flexibility and rapid iteration cycle in LLM-based workflows, creating a need for a practical protocol that constrains researcher degrees of freedom without halting methodological innovation.
AI.method: The authors define a preregistration-based protocol tailored to LLM studies: (1) design and finalize the experimental procedure and analysis plan using currently available models; (2) preregister this plan along with a list or criteria of eligible future models; (3) once preregistration is locked in, run the confirmatory experiment on the first eligible LLM released afterward. They then test this protocol on two benchmark tasks with known ground truth using 20 models from four providers and 11 distinct LLM-analysis configurations that can be p-hacked on earlier models. They simulate the protocol to measure how often a configuration that successfully p-hacks one model also “transfers” its hack to newer models and thus would escape the protocol’s protection. They further run robustness and “stress test” analyses and finally implement their own protocol in a preregistered confirmatory study on a subsequently released model.
AI.result: Simulation across 20 LLMs and 11 analysis configurations shows that the proposed protocol would have prevented successful transfer of p-hacked configurations in about three-quarters of cases: 73.9% in one task and 72.7% in the other. Stress tests indicate that even under more challenging assumptions, the reduction in effective p-hacking remains substantial. In the real preregistered follow-up, 7 configurations that had successfully p-hacked the prior model were evaluated on the next eligible released model; in 6 of these 7 cases, the previously successful p-hacks no longer worked, corroborating the protocol’s predicted effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: A simple preregistration protocol that commits researchers to using the first eligible future LLM after design-time meaningfully reduces opportunities for p-hacking in LLM-based research. Because hacking strategies often fail to generalize across model generations, tying confirmatory analyses to unreleased models that cannot be tuned against provides a practical, implementable safeguard. The findings suggest that as LLMs rapidly evolve, methodological standards based on preregistration and future-model commitment can enhance the credibility and robustness of empirical work that depends on LLM outputs.

title: Verifiable Geometry Problem Solving: Solver-Driven Autoformalization and Theorem Proposing
summary: Geometry Problem Solving have increasingly adopt the neuro-symbolic paradigm, combining neural intuition with symbolic rigor. However, current frameworks suffer from severe bottlenecks in two core stages: autoformalization, which treats multimodal translation as a static task decoupled from downstream solver compatibility, and theorem prediction, where solvers frequently hit a deductive impasse due to fixed rule libraries. To address these, we propose SD-GPS, a solver-driven framework that treats the symbolic solver as an execution oracle throughout both formalization and deduction. First, Solver-Driven Autoformalization unifies supervised formal-language adaptation and solvability-guided reinforcement learning into a single module built on QwenVL3-2B, making executability the central training signal. Second, Verified Theorem Proposing introduces an impasse-aware agent that proposes local auxiliary lemmas from current proof states, ensuring soundness by filtering all proposals through symbolic verification. Empirical evaluations on Geometry3K and PGPS9K demonstrate that SD-GPS consistently outperforms existing MLLM, neural, and neuro-symbolic methods across standard completion, multiple-choice, and cross-modal reference regimes, proving that closing the loop between multimodal perception and symbolic execution significantly improves geometric reasoning, offering profound insights into how neural agents can be grounded by formal systems to achieve verifiable problem-solving capabilities.

AI.tldr: SD-GPS is a solver-driven neuro-symbolic framework that tightly couples multimodal autoformalization with symbolic deduction to improve geometry problem solving.
AI.motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic geometry solvers struggle with two bottlenecks: (1) autoformalization is treated as a one-shot translation from multimodal input to formal language, ignoring whether the resulting formalization is actually executable by the downstream symbolic solver; (2) theorem prediction relies on a fixed rule or lemma library, so solvers often reach deductive dead ends when necessary auxiliary lemmas are missing. The paper aims to close the loop between perception and symbolic execution so that the symbolic solver guides both the formation of formal statements and the exploration of proofs, leading to more reliable, verifiable reasoning.
AI.method: The authors introduce SD-GPS, a solver-driven geometry problem solving framework with two main components. (1) Solver-Driven Autoformalization: they build on QwenVL3-2B to translate multimodal problem statements (text plus diagrams) into formal representations. This module is trained using a combination of supervised learning for adapting to the target formal language and reinforcement learning where the reward is based on downstream solvability by the symbolic solver, making executability a key training signal. (2) Verified Theorem Proposing: they add an impasse-aware agent that, upon detecting that the solver is stuck, proposes local auxiliary lemmas based on the current proof state. Every proposed lemma is filtered through a symbolic verification step to ensure logical soundness before being accepted and used to continue deduction.
AI.result: On the Geometry3K and PGPS9K benchmarks, SD-GPS achieves better performance than prior MLLM-based, purely neural, and other neuro-symbolic methods. The improvements hold across several evaluation regimes, including standard proof completion, multiple-choice settings, and cross-modal reference tasks, indicating the robustness and generality of the approach.
AI.conclusion: By using the symbolic solver as an execution oracle in both the formalization and reasoning phases, SD-GPS closes the loop between multimodal perception and symbolic execution. This tight integration leads to stronger geometric reasoning performance and demonstrates that grounding neural components in formal systems with verification can yield more reliable, verifiable problem-solving agents.

title: Mitigating Position Bias in Transformers via Layer-Specific Positional Embedding Scaling
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) still struggle with the ``lost-in-the-middle'' problem, where critical information located in the middle of long-context inputs is often underrepresented or lost. While existing methods attempt to address this by combining multi-scale rotary position embeddings (RoPE), they typically suffer from high latency or rely on suboptimal hand-crafted scaling strategies. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a layer-specific positional embedding scaling~(LPES) method that assigns distinct scaling factors to each layer. LPES achieves a more balanced attention distribution without fine-tuning model parameters or increasing inference delay. A specially designed genetic algorithm is employed to efficiently select the optimal scaling factors for each layer by incorporating Bézier curves to significantly reduce the search space. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LPES effectively mitigates positional attention bias and delivers consistent improvements across multiple long-context benchmarks, yielding up to an $11.2$\% accuracy gain on the key-value retrieval dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LPES, a layer-specific positional embedding scaling method for LLMs that alleviates the lost-in-the-middle problem without model fine-tuning or added latency, achieving notable accuracy gains on long-context tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit the lost-in-the-middle problem, where information in the middle of long inputs is poorly attended to. Existing fixes that modify rotary position embeddings either slow inference or rely on hand-crafted, suboptimal scaling schemes. A better approach is needed to rebalance attention across long contexts efficiently and automatically.
AI.method: The authors introduce Layer-Specific Positional Embedding Scaling (LPES), which assigns unique RoPE scaling factors to each transformer layer. Instead of changing model weights, they adapt only positional scaling. To find good scaling configurations, they design a genetic algorithm search procedure that uses Bézier curves to parameterize and restrict the space of possible layer-wise scaling patterns, making optimization more efficient. This produces an optimized set of per-layer scaling factors that can be applied at inference time.
AI.result: LPES reduces positional attention bias and mitigates the lost-in-the-middle effect in LLMs. Experiments across several long-context benchmarks, including key-value retrieval, show consistent performance gains, with up to 11.2% accuracy improvement on the key-value retrieval dataset compared with baselines.
AI.conclusion: Layer-wise adaptive scaling of positional embeddings is an effective, low-overhead way to improve long-context performance in LLMs. LPES can be plugged into existing RoPE-based models without fine-tuning or added latency, offering a practical solution to the lost-in-the-middle problem and suggesting that per-layer positional treatment is a key factor in long-context reasoning.

title: RelBall: Relation Ball with Quaternion Rotation for Knowledge Graph Completion
summary: Real-world knowledge graphs are often incomplete, lacking many valid facts. Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) aims to predict missing links using known triples, thereby enhancing graph coverage. A key challenge is modeling diverse relational patterns such as symmetry, antisymmetry, inversion, composition and semantic hierarchy. Existing models such as RotatE can capture symmetric, antisymmetric, inverse, and commutative composition patterns, yet struggle with non-commutative composition. Rotate3D addresses this by introducing non-commutativity via three-dimensional rotations, but still fails to capture the semantic hierarchies prevalent in knowledge graphs. Moreover, both models cannot effectively model one-to-many relations. To overcome these limitations, we propose RelBall, which extends Rotate3D with two innovations. First, our model introduces modulus transformation to model hierarchies, driving abstract concepts toward smaller moduli and concrete instances toward larger ones. Second, it introduces a tail-centric relation ball to model one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relations. RelBall offers the following advantages: (1) coverage of all relational patterns, including the ones mentioned above; (2) an interpretable hierarchical representation where the modulus directly reflect semantic levels; (3) support for one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relations. Experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate RelBall's competitive link prediction performance against various baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RelBall, a new knowledge graph embedding model that extends Rotate3D to model more relational patterns (including hierarchies and various many-valued relations) using modulus transformation and tail-centric relation balls, achieving competitive link prediction performance.
AI.motivation: Real-world knowledge graphs are incomplete and require Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) to infer missing links. Existing models like RotatE and Rotate3D can capture several relational patterns (symmetry, antisymmetry, inversion, some compositions), but they fail with non-commutative composition, semantic hierarchies, and especially one-to-many / many-to-one relations. There is a need for a model that jointly handles all these patterns while offering interpretable hierarchical representations.
AI.method: The authors extend the Rotate3D model to a new framework called RelBall. They keep the rotational mechanism for non-commutative composition and add two key components: (1) a modulus transformation that pushes more abstract concepts to smaller embedding moduli and more concrete instances to larger moduli, thereby encoding semantic hierarchies; and (2) a tail-centric relation ball, i.e., a ball-shaped region in embedding space centered around a transformed tail representation, which allows flexible modeling of one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relation cardinalities. Together, these components allow RelBall to cover a wide range of relational patterns while maintaining geometric interpretability.
AI.result: RelBall is empirically evaluated on multiple standard knowledge graph datasets for link prediction. The experiments show that RelBall achieves competitive or superior performance compared to strong baselines, including RotatE and Rotate3D, in terms of standard metrics such as link prediction accuracy. The results also indicate that RelBall successfully models hierarchical patterns and complex relation cardinalities that prior methods struggle with.
AI.conclusion: RelBall effectively addresses the limitations of prior KGC models by jointly modeling non-commutative composition, semantic hierarchies, and diverse relation cardinalities. Its modulus-based hierarchy encodes semantic levels in an interpretable way, while the tail-centric relation ball allows flexible handling of one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relations. The reported experiments confirm that RelBall provides broad relational pattern coverage and competitive link prediction performance, making it a strong candidate for improving knowledge graph completion in practice.

title: Low-Agreeableness Persona Conditioning for Safe LLM Fine-Tuning
summary: Recent work has shown that fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for social warmth degrades factual reliability and increases sycophancy. We investigate a related but distinct failure mode: warmth fine-tuning also weakens adversarial safety, making models more susceptible to jailbreaks and harmful output generation. We examine whether this reflects an inherent consequence of empathetic adaptation or an artifact of data construction. To address this, we introduce a persona-driven rewriting pipeline that conditions user turns on low agreeableness and pairs this with warm, de-escalating assistant responses. Across three experiments on four models, our approach reduces jailbreak susceptibility and harmful output rates relative to generic warmth fine-tuning baselines, while preserving conversational warmth. Representational probing provides suggestive evidence that this conditioning reduces the geometric alignment between warmth and compliance directions in latent space. These results show that safer empathetic fine-tuning is achievable through data design alone, without safety labels, harm detectors, or changes to the training objective.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that naively fine-tuning LLMs for social warmth harms adversarial safety, but this can be mitigated by better data design that keeps conversations warm while reducing jailbreak vulnerability, without extra safety labels or objective changes.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs to sound warmer and more empathetic tends to make them less factually reliable, more sycophantic, and more vulnerable to jailbreaks and harmful outputs. It is unclear whether this trade-off is inevitable or caused by how warmth data are constructed. The paper aims to disentangle these causes and find a way to obtain empathetic yet safe models.
AI.method: The authors study adversarial safety degradation from warmth fine-tuning and propose a persona-driven rewriting pipeline. In this pipeline, user turns are rewritten to express low agreeableness (more challenging or disagreeable), while assistant responses remain warm and de-escalating. They fine-tune four LLMs with this data and compare against standard warmth fine-tuning baselines using three experimental setups. They also use representational probing to analyze how the latent space geometry (alignment between warmth and compliance directions) changes after training.
AI.result: Models trained with the persona-driven, low-agreeableness user / warm-assistant data are less susceptible to jailbreaks and produce harmful outputs less often than those trained with generic warmth fine-tuning, while maintaining similar levels of perceived conversational warmth. Probing suggests that the latent representation of warmth becomes less geometrically aligned with compliance, indicating that the model can be warm without being over-compliant to all user demands.
AI.conclusion: Empathetic fine-tuning need not inherently weaken adversarial safety. By carefully designing training data—specifically, using low-agreeableness user personas paired with warm, de-escalating assistant replies—LLMs can be made both warm and safer against jailbreaks, without needing explicit safety labels, harm detectors, or modified training objectives. Data design alone can decouple warmth from unsafe over-compliance.

title: Lifted Causal Inference
comment: Accepted to the Annals of Mathematics and Artificial Intelligence journal
summary: Lifted inference exploits indistinguishabilities in probabilistic graphical models by using a representative for indistinguishable objects, thereby speeding up query answering while maintaining exact answers. In this article, we show how lifting can be applied to efficiently compute causal effects in relational domains. More specifically, we introduce parametric causal factor graphs (PCFGs) to incorporate causal knowledge in lifted models and give a formal semantics of interventions therein. We further present the Lifted Causal Inference (LCI) algorithm to compute causal effects on a lifted level, thereby drastically speeding up causal inference compared to propositional inference, e.g., in causal Bayesian networks. In addition, we present partially directed parametric causal factor graphs (PD-PCFGs) as a generalisation of PCFGs to handle partial causal knowledge and extend LCI to perform lifted causal inference in a PD-PCFG, thereby extending the applicability of lifted causal inference to a broader range of models requiring less prior knowledge about causal relationships.

AI.tldr: They extend lifted probabilistic inference to causal inference in relational models, introducing new graph formalisms and algorithms that compute causal effects much more efficiently than propositional methods.
AI.motivation: Causal inference in relational and probabilistic graphical models is typically done at the propositional level, which becomes computationally expensive as domains grow. Lifted inference has shown that exploiting symmetries/indistinguishabilities can give large speedups for probabilistic queries, but this has not been fully leveraged for causal effect computation, especially when only partial causal knowledge is available. The paper aims to bring the efficiency benefits of lifted inference to causal inference, and to support settings where the causal structure is not fully known.
AI.method: 1) Define Parametric Causal Factor Graphs (PCFGs), extending lifted probabilistic models with explicit causal structure and a formal semantics of interventions. 2) Develop the Lifted Causal Inference (LCI) algorithm, which computes causal effects directly on the lifted representation instead of grounding to a propositional model. 3) Generalize to Partially Directed Parametric Causal Factor Graphs (PD-PCFGs) that encode partial causal knowledge via partially directed edges. 4) Extend LCI to work with PD-PCFGs, enabling lifted causal inference under partially specified causal structures.
AI.result: They show that LCI can compute causal effects on PCFGs and PD-PCFGs while remaining exact, and that it achieves large computational speedups compared to standard propositional causal inference methods such as those based on causal Bayesian networks. The extended framework handles both full and partial causal knowledge in relational domains.
AI.conclusion: Lifted inference principles can be successfully extended from probabilistic to causal reasoning in relational settings. The introduced PCFG and PD-PCFG formalisms, together with the LCI algorithm and its extension, enable efficient and exact computation of causal effects, significantly reducing complexity versus propositional approaches and broadening applicability to cases with incomplete causal knowledge.

title: Do Speech Emphasis Models Generalize across Languages and Emotions?
summary: Prosodic emphasis varies across languages, emotions, and speaking styles, yet existing emphasis detection models are largely trained and evaluated on monolingual neutral read speech. We introduce MMEE (Multilingual Multi-Emotion Emphasis), a corpus of 10,000 professionally recorded expressive utterances (14.13 hours) across 7 languages and 34 emotion/style categories, with three-level perceptual labels (10 annotations per sample). We benchmark two state-of-the-art architectures under monolingual, cross-lingual, multilingual, cross-emotion, cross-dataset, and data-scale settings. Monolingual models show limited zero-shot transfer, degrading across typologically distant languages, while multilingual training substantially improves robustness. Models transfer robustly between high- and low-arousal emotions; bidirectional transfer between synthetic and perceptual benchmarks suggests shared prosodic structure; and performance stays robust even at smaller training scales.

AI.tldr: They build a new multilingual, multi-emotion emphasis dataset and show multilingual training gives more robust emphasis detection than monolingual models, with good transfer across emotions, datasets, and data scales.
AI.motivation: Existing prosodic emphasis detection models are mostly trained and evaluated on monolingual, neutral, carefully read speech, which does not match the diversity of real-world speech across languages, emotions, and speaking styles. There is a need for a benchmark that covers multiple languages and emotional styles, and for a systematic study of how well emphasis detection models transfer across these variations.
AI.method: They construct MMEE, a corpus of 10,000 professionally recorded utterances (14.13 hours) covering 7 different languages and 34 emotion/style categories. Each utterance receives three-level emphasis labels obtained via perception with 10 annotations per sample. Using this corpus, they benchmark two state-of-the-art emphasis detection architectures under various training and evaluation setups: monolingual, cross-lingual, multilingual, cross-emotion, cross-dataset, and different training data scales. They then analyze how performance changes across these settings.
AI.result: Monolingual models perform poorly in zero-shot transfer, especially when applied to typologically distant languages. In contrast, models trained on multilingual data show considerably improved robustness across languages. The models also transfer well between high- and low-arousal emotional categories. There is bidirectional transfer between synthetic and perceptual benchmarks, indicating they share underlying prosodic patterns. Additionally, the models maintain strong performance even when trained on smaller subsets of the data, suggesting efficiency and robustness to reduced training scale.
AI.conclusion: MMEE provides a rich, multilingual, multi-emotion benchmark for prosodic emphasis detection and reveals that multilingual training substantially enhances robustness and cross-condition generalization compared to monolingual training. The observed robust transfer across emotions, datasets, and data scales, as well as between synthetic and perceptual benchmarks, implies that prosodic emphasis has shared, learnable structure that extends across languages and expressive styles.

title: JD Oxygen AI Item Center (Oxygen AIIC) V1: An Industrial-Scale LLM/VLM-Centric Solution for Item Understanding, Management, and Applications
summary: JD.com, one of the world's largest e-commerce platforms, serves over 700 million active users and millions of merchants, with a catalog of tens of billions of SKUs. At this scale, high-quality, structured item knowledge underpins a better consumer experience, lower management costs, and higher operational efficiency-yet producing and serving it poses three industrial-scale challenges: fast-emerging concepts, high-quality knowledge production for massive SKUs, and diverse downstream requirements. To address these challenges, we present the JD Oxygen AI Item Center (Oxygen AIIC), an industrial-scale platform built on LLMs/VLMs for item-knowledge production and service. Oxygen AIIC is built around four core pillars: (i) ontology engineering driven by efficient human-AI collaboration, which supports the dynamic evolution and agile expansion of an ontology with millions of entries; (ii) a "Semantic Search then Discrimination"(S2D) knowledge identification architecture that, combined with throughput improvement strategies, enables scalable, extensible, and high-throughput AI Item Library production for tens of billions of SKUs; (iii) self-evolving item-understanding LLMs/VLMs that improve in a stable and controllable manner, enabling knowledge production with 94.2% precision and 82.8% recall; and (iv) a unified item tunnel that serves as the data and service hub. Oxygen AIIC now covers tens of thousands of JD categories and processes hundreds of millions of item updates per day on Huawei Ascend NPUs. It has accumulated hundreds of billions of item-knowledge assets. Deployed across core business scenarios-including search, recommendation, operations, category planning-Oxygen AIIC has delivered measurable gains at scale. Search-traffic coverage reaches 80.4%, item-information quality issues drop by 37%, the automated fill rate of core attributes during item listing exceeds 80%.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Oxygen AIIC, JD.com's industrial-scale LLM/VLM-based platform for generating and serving high-quality structured item knowledge across tens of billions of SKUs, significantly improving search, recommendation, and operational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Managing high-quality, structured item knowledge at JD.com’s massive scale (hundreds of millions of users, millions of merchants, tens of billions of SKUs) is difficult due to rapidly emerging concepts, the need for accurate knowledge across all items, and diverse requirements from downstream applications such as search and recommendation. The paper is motivated by the need to industrialize and automate item-knowledge production and serving while maintaining high precision and recall.
AI.method: The paper introduces Oxygen AIIC, an LLM/VLM-based platform built on four pillars: (1) ontology engineering with human-AI collaboration for dynamic, large-scale ontology evolution; (2) a “Semantic Search then Discrimination” (S2D) architecture for scalable knowledge identification and AI Item Library construction, enhanced by throughput-boosting strategies; (3) self-evolving item-understanding LLMs/VLMs that can be updated in a controlled fashion to improve performance; and (4) a unified item tunnel that integrates data and services, enabling efficient knowledge serving to multiple business scenarios.
AI.result: Oxygen AIIC covers tens of thousands of product categories and processes hundreds of millions of item updates daily on Huawei Ascend NPUs, accumulating hundreds of billions of item-knowledge assets. It achieves 94.2% precision and 82.8% recall in knowledge production. In online business metrics, it reaches 80.4% search-traffic coverage, reduces item-information quality issues by 37%, and increases automated filling of core attributes during item listing to over 80%.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Oxygen AIIC successfully addresses industrial-scale challenges in item-knowledge production and serving for ultra-large e-commerce platforms. By combining LLM/VLM technologies with scalable architectures and human-AI collaboration, it delivers high-quality, high-coverage item knowledge that translates into significant, measurable improvements across JD.com’s core business functions.

title: Enhancing Numerical Prediction in LLMs via Smooth MMD Alignment
summary: Despite their strong general capabilities, large language models (LLMs) often remain unreliable when outputs must be numerically precise. A key reason is the training objective: standard cross-entropy treats numeric tokens as unstructured categories and ignores the metric structure of their values. We address this mismatch with Smooth Maximum Mean Discrepancy (SMMD), which builds on the classic MMD by incorporating value-distance kernels over numeric tokens and graph-based smoothness. With this kernel defined over a numeric sub-vocabulary, SMMD aligns the predicted numeric distribution to the target via kernel matching and smooths the prediction-target residual over the induced kernel graph to encourage local consistency. We evaluate SMMD on four numeric-target tasks: mathematical reasoning, arithmetic calculation, clock-time recognition, and chart question answering, across multiple open-weight LLM and VLM backbones. SMMD consistently improves accuracy over both cross-entropy and recent numeric-target losses; analyses show complementary effects between MMD and smoothness and underscore the importance of distance-based kernel design. Code is available at https://github.com/Zuozhuo/smmd-loss.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new training loss, Smooth Maximum Mean Discrepancy (SMMD), to make LLMs more numerically precise by respecting numeric value distances.
AI.motivation: Standard cross-entropy loss treats numeric tokens as unrelated categories, ignoring that numbers have an inherent metric structure (e.g., 3 is closer to 4 than to 100). This leads LLMs to be unreliable on tasks needing exact or near-exact numeric outputs, such as arithmetic or reading values from charts. The authors aim to design a loss that directly leverages numeric distances to improve numeric reasoning and prediction.
AI.method: They design Smooth Maximum Mean Discrepancy (SMMD), a loss built on Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD). First, they define a distance-based kernel over numeric tokens in a numeric sub-vocabulary so that the loss can compare predicted and target numeric distributions based on value distances. Then, they introduce graph-based smoothness over the kernel-induced graph, smoothing the residuals between prediction and target across neighboring numeric values to enforce local consistency (numbers close in value should have similar prediction behavior). The overall loss aligns prediction and target via kernel matching (MMD term) and enforces local smoothness (graph term).
AI.result: On four tasks with numeric targets—mathematical reasoning, arithmetic calculation, clock-time recognition, and chart question answering—applied to several open-weight LLM and VLM backbones, SMMD consistently yields higher accuracy than both standard cross-entropy and recent specialized numeric-target losses. Ablation and analysis show that the MMD and smoothness components provide complementary gains, and that well-designed, distance-based kernels are crucial to performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating numeric value distances into the training loss via SMMD significantly improves the numerical reliability of LLMs and VLMs across diverse tasks. Treating numbers with a metric-aware objective—combining kernel-based distribution matching and graph smoothness—offers a principled and empirically effective alternative to pure cross-entropy for numeric prediction problems.

title: Ontology-Guided Evidence Path Inference for Multi-hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering
summary: Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) aims to answer natural-language questions by reasoning over structured facts. Existing multi-hop KGQA methods mainly rely on topic-centered expansion, which faces two key challenges: the search space rapidly grows with noisy mixed-type paths, and retrieved paths may fail to satisfy the semantic constraints of complex questions. To address these challenges, we propose OPI, an ontology-guided evidence path inference framework for multi-hop KGQA. OPI introduces a relation-centric ontology graph to capture the head-tail type constraints of relations, providing a compact interface for answer-side constraints. Based on this ontology graph, OPI first introduces a bidirectional retrieval mechanism by mapping the predicted answer type to compatible final-hop relations and combining topic-side prefix expansion with answer-side final-hop matching, thereby suppressing noisy mixed-type expansion. OPI further adopts an iterative refinement strategy to reassess retrieved paths and candidate answers under the question context, filtering type-compatible but question-irrelevant evidence for more reliable answer prediction. Experiments on WebQSP, CWQ, and MetaQA show that OPI substantially reduces the search space, improves Hit@1/F1 by 4.6/5.0 points on WebQSP and 8.9/3.3 points on CWQ over the strongest prior results, and achieves near-saturated Hit@1 on MetaQA with the retrieval module alone.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes OPI, an ontology-guided, relation-centric framework that prunes the KGQA search space and refines evidence paths using both topic and answer-side constraints, leading to significantly better multi-hop QA accuracy.
AI.motivation: Multi-hop KGQA requires reasoning over multiple hops in a large knowledge graph, but existing topic-centered expansion strategies cause an explosion in search space, introduce many noisy and mixed-type paths, and often violate implicit semantic/type constraints in complex questions. There is a need for a principled way to incorporate type and relation constraints so that path retrieval is both efficient and better aligned with question semantics.
AI.method: OPI builds a relation-centric ontology graph that encodes head-tail type constraints for relations, functioning as a compact representation of type compatibility. Using this ontology graph, it performs bidirectional retrieval: (1) predicts the answer type from the question and maps it to compatible final-hop relations on the answer side, and (2) carries out traditional topic-side prefix expansion from detected topic entities. These two directions are combined so that only paths whose final hops are type-compatible with the predicted answer type are considered, thus reducing noisy mixed-type expansions. Then, OPI applies an iterative refinement mechanism that reevaluates the retrieved reasoning paths and candidate answers under the full question context, filtering out paths that are type-compatible but semantically irrelevant.
AI.result: On three benchmarks—WebQSP, CWQ, and MetaQA—OPI significantly reduces the path search space while improving answer accuracy. It improves Hit@1/F1 by 4.6/5.0 points on WebQSP and 8.9/3.3 points on CWQ compared with the previous strongest methods. On MetaQA, its retrieval module alone attains nearly saturated Hit@1, indicating that the ontology-guided retrieval is already sufficient for high performance on that dataset.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating a relation-centric ontology graph into multi-hop KGQA allows more effective control of type and semantic constraints during path retrieval. By combining bidirectional (topic-side and answer-side) search with iterative context-aware refinement, OPI can both shrink the search space and improve answer precision, outperforming prior KGQA approaches across multiple benchmarks and demonstrating the value of ontology-guided evidence path inference.

title: KG2Cypher: Data-Centric Pipeline for Building Enterprise Text-to-Cypher Systems
summary: Enterprise Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are increasingly used for internal search, analytics, and question answering, but building natural-language interfaces for private enterprise graphs remains costly. We present KG2Cypher, a data-centric pipeline for building enterprise text-to-Cypher systems from existing KGs. KG2Cypher first constructs an executable Cypher query from observed graph facts and then uses LLMs to generate its associated natural-language question. The resulting Text-Cypher pairs are validated with an LLM judge and human validation, and are converted into candidate-aware SFT data. The trained generator is served with class-conditioned schema prompting, entity retrieval, and LoRA-based inference. We evaluate KG2Cypher in Korean enterprise settings, where short search-style queries and schema paraphrases make language grounding difficult. LoRA SFT improves execution-result F1 from 0.806 to 0.950 on broadcast-program queries and from 0.70 to 0.92 on company queries. In an 11-class setting, KG2Cypher achieves 95.2% exact match, 99.9% execution rate, and 0.964 execution-result F1.

AI.tldr: KG2Cypher is a data-centric pipeline that auto-generates high-quality text-to-Cypher training data from existing enterprise knowledge graphs and trains an LLM-based query generator for natural-language interfaces.
AI.motivation: Building natural-language interfaces for private enterprise knowledge graphs is expensive and difficult, especially in settings like Korean enterprises where queries are short, search-style, and schemas are heavily paraphrased, making language-to-schema grounding challenging. There is a need for a scalable way to create high-quality text-to-Cypher systems without extensive manual annotation.
AI.method: KG2Cypher starts from existing enterprise KGs and automatically constructs executable Cypher queries based on observed graph facts. Large language models are then used to generate corresponding natural-language questions for these queries, yielding Text-Cypher pairs. These pairs are filtered and validated using an LLM judge and human annotators and then converted into candidate-aware supervised fine-tuning data. A generator model is trained and deployed with class-conditioned schema prompts, entity retrieval mechanisms, and LoRA-based fine-tuning and inference for efficiency.
AI.result: In Korean enterprise scenarios (broadcast program and company KGs), LoRA-based supervised fine-tuning significantly boosts execution-result F1: from 0.806 to 0.950 on broadcast-program queries and from 0.70 to 0.92 on company queries. In a more complex 11-class configuration, KG2Cypher attains 95.2% exact-match accuracy, 99.9% query execution rate, and 0.964 execution-result F1, demonstrating robust query generation quality and grounding.
AI.conclusion: KG2Cypher provides an effective data-centric approach to building text-to-Cypher systems over enterprise knowledge graphs, substantially reducing the need for manual annotation while delivering high execution accuracy. By generating and validating Text-Cypher pairs from existing graph data and leveraging LoRA-based fine-tuning with schema-aware prompting and entity retrieval, it achieves strong performance even in challenging Korean enterprise settings.

title: AI-Driven Synthesis for High-Tech System Design: Automating Innovation
summary: This article addresses the combinatorial complexity inherent in modern high-tech system design by presenting automation-in-design (AiD) as a transformative paradigm. We propose computational design synthesis (CDS), a framework utilising deep learning and generative AI to automate the creation of novel systems. Two case studies (e-drive system design and spatial dimensioning problem) serve as proof-points for this approach. The AI-driven methods used in the case studies represent a fundamental shift in engineering, advancing from simulation-based optimisation towards autonomous design with minimal human supervision.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Automation-in-Design (AiD) and a computational design synthesis (CDS) framework using deep learning and generative AI to automate complex engineering system design, demonstrated via two case studies.
AI.motivation: Modern high-tech systems have massive combinatorial design spaces that are too complex for traditional manual or simulation-based optimisation methods, creating a need for more autonomous, AI-driven design approaches.
AI.method: Introduce the Automation-in-Design (AiD) paradigm and define the Computational Design Synthesis (CDS) framework that leverages deep learning and generative AI models to automatically generate and evaluate design concepts. The approach is empirically illustrated with two engineering case studies: electric drive (e-drive) system design and a spatial dimensioning problem.
AI.result: The AI-driven CDS framework successfully generates viable design solutions for both the e-drive system and the spatial dimensioning case, showing that deep learning and generative AI can handle complex design tasks with limited human intervention.
AI.conclusion: Automation-in-Design, operationalised through the CDS framework, marks a shift from classical simulation-based optimisation toward more autonomous, AI-driven engineering design processes, suggesting that future engineering workflows will rely increasingly on generative AI for initial concept creation and exploration of large design spaces.

title: Output-Space Allocation Costs for Calibration-Guided LLM Compression: An Empirical Study
summary: Training-free compression methods for large language models (LLMs) often use calibration data to guide compression decisions. ROCKET, a recent method combining sparse-dictionary factorization with multi-choice knapsack problem (MCKP) allocation, derives its per-layer factorization from an output reconstruction objective but uses weight-space Frobenius error as the MCKP allocation cost. We investigate whether aligning the allocation cost with the output-space objective improves compressed model fidelity. On Qwen3-8B at 50\% compression, our ROCKET-ActCost achieves +0.8 percentage points higher average accuracy across 8 zero-shot benchmarks (53.1\% vs 52.3\%), but increases WikiText perplexity by 16\% (61.46 vs 52.98). This accuracy-perplexity tradeoff reveals that different allocation objectives favor different downstream metrics. The high correlation ($>$0.99) between weight-space and output-space errors limits allocation divergence, explaining the modest effect size. On Llama-3.2-1B at 20\% compression, the two methods produce near-identical results (53.3\% vs 53.5\% accuracy, 14.45 vs 14.66 PPL), suggesting that the effect of the cost function is minor at lower compression ratios.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether using an output-space error as the allocation cost in ROCKET-style training-free LLM compression improves downstream performance, finding small, metric-dependent gains at high compression and negligible effects at lower compression due to strong correlation between weight- and output-space errors.
AI.motivation: Existing training-free compression methods for LLMs like ROCKET use calibration data to guide compression, deriving factorization from an output reconstruction objective but still relying on weight-space Frobenius norm as the allocation cost in the multi-choice knapsack problem. This mismatch may be suboptimal if the final goal is to preserve output behavior and downstream accuracy. The authors are motivated to test whether aligning the allocation cost with the output-space objective can provide better compressed model fidelity, especially at substantial compression ratios.
AI.method: They modify ROCKET by replacing the weight-space Frobenius error used as the allocation cost in the multi-choice knapsack problem with an output-space error that matches the layer-wise output reconstruction objective, yielding a variant they call ROCKET-ActCost. They then compare original ROCKET and ROCKET-ActCost on two LLMs (Qwen3-8B at 50% compression and Llama-3.2-1B at 20% compression), evaluating both zero-shot benchmark accuracies across eight tasks and language modeling perplexity on WikiText. They also analyze the correlation between weight-space and output-space errors to understand how much the allocation actually changes.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B at 50% compression, ROCKET-ActCost improves average zero-shot accuracy by 0.8 percentage points (53.1% vs. 52.3%) relative to the original ROCKET, but at the cost of a 16% increase in WikiText perplexity (61.46 vs. 52.98). On Llama-3.2-1B at 20% compression, the two methods yield almost identical performance in both accuracy (53.3% vs. 53.5%) and perplexity (14.45 vs. 14.66). They find that the correlation between weight-space and output-space errors is greater than 0.99, which means that changing the cost function only slightly alters allocation decisions, limiting impact.
AI.conclusion: Aligning the MCKP allocation cost with the output-space reconstruction objective can modestly improve zero-shot accuracy at high compression but introduces a tradeoff by worsening perplexity, indicating that different allocation objectives favor different evaluation metrics. Due to the very high correlation between weight-space and output-space errors, the resulting allocations remain very similar, which explains the small effect sizes. At lower compression ratios, the choice of allocation cost has negligible impact, suggesting that further gains may require fundamentally different objectives or compression strategies rather than simply changing the cost metric.

title: Tandem Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has significantly improved the reasoning capability of large language models, reaching expert or even superhuman performance in domains such as competition math. However, whether weaker agents and humans can actually harness this capability is far less certain, with RLVR documented to drift reasoning toward idiosyncratic patterns such as poor readability and language mixing. Tandem training is a recently introduced paradigm that targets this compatibility problem: a trained, stronger senior co-generates each rollout with a frozen, weaker junior, and the two are rewarded as a team, so the senior is pushed to reason in ways the junior can follow. Yet this paradigm has so far been demonstrated only in proof-of-concept settings, leaving open whether it scales to the long chains of thought of the modern RLVR pipeline. In this work, we propose Tandem Reinforcement Learning (TRL), which carries the tandem training paradigm into RLVR. In TRL, the senior and a frozen junior alternate stochastically to co-generate the reasoning, the resulting generation is rewarded, and the standard GRPO loss is applied to the senior. Training Qwen3-4B-Instruct on competition math, we find that TRL matches vanilla GRPO on solo reasoning capability while three properties emerge together from the same rollout structure: stronger handoff robustness with the junior, reduced distributional drift from the junior, and a chain-of-thought more legible to the junior. Our results demonstrate a promising route for RLVR with practical payoffs in multi-model communication and human compatibility.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Tandem Reinforcement Learning (TRL), a method that integrates tandem training into RL with verifiable rewards to keep strong models aligned with weaker models’ reasoning while preserving high performance.
AI.motivation: RL with verifiable rewards greatly boosts large language models’ reasoning, but produces idiosyncratic, hard-to-follow chains of thought that are poorly compatible with weaker models and humans. Existing tandem training work suggests a way to improve compatibility, but has only been shown in toy settings and not in full RLVR pipelines with long reasoning chains. The paper aims to scale this idea to realistic settings so that strong models remain usable collaborators for weaker models and humans.
AI.method: They propose TRL, where a strong “senior” model and a frozen weaker “junior” model stochastically alternate to co-generate a single chain-of-thought. The final mixed reasoning trace is scored by a verifiable reward, and a standard GRPO RL objective is applied only to the senior’s actions. This pushes the senior to reason in ways that work well when interleaved with the junior, encouraging compatibility without explicitly training the junior.
AI.result: Using Qwen3-4B-Instruct on competition math, TRL achieves similar solo problem-solving performance to vanilla GRPO-trained models, while simultaneously improving: (1) robustness when control is handed off to the junior mid-reasoning, (2) closeness of the senior’s reasoning distribution to that of the junior, and (3) legibility of the senior’s chain-of-thought to the junior model.
AI.conclusion: TRL effectively extends tandem training to full RLVR setups, preserving expert-level reasoning performance while improving cross-model compatibility and interpretability of chains-of-thought. This suggests a practical path for building powerful RLVR-trained models that remain usable for weaker models and, by analogy, for humans in multi-agent and human–AI collaboration scenarios.

title: SHIFT: Gate-Modulated Activation Steering for Knowledge Conflict Mitigation in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enhances LLMs by incorporating external knowledge to support response generation. However, conflicts between retrieved context and parametric knowledge have emerged as a critical challenge in RAG systems. To mitigate such conflicts, numerous studies have attempted to identify and edit knowledge-related internal neurons, aiming to improve the ability of LLMs to rely on contextual evidence during generation. However, these neuron-level approaches may introduce unintended cascading effects that compromise the general capabilities of LLMs, as the modified neurons are often entangled with broader model behaviors and functionalities. In this paper, we introduce SHIFT, a novel framework that reformulates neuron-level modification as learnable gate modulation, allowing LLMs to adaptively regulate internal activations for knowledge conflict resolution. Technically, our SHIFT equips LLMs with a lightweight gate module and optimizes fewer than 0.01% trainable parameters while keeping the backbone model frozen. During generation, the gate module adjusts the model's internal representations to adaptively leverage contextual and parametric knowledge. Extensive experiments on six datasets validate the effectiveness of our SHIFT in comparison with various competing baselines. All datasets and code are available at https://github.com/OpenBMB/SHIFT.

AI.tldr: SHIFT is a lightweight, trainable gating framework that adaptively modulates internal LLM activations to resolve conflicts between retrieved and parametric knowledge in RAG, without editing neurons or fine-tuning the whole model.
AI.motivation: RAG systems often face conflicts between retrieved context and an LLM’s built-in (parametric) knowledge. Prior work tries to directly edit or identify knowledge neurons to force models to rely more on context, but these neuron-level edits can have unintended side effects, degrading general abilities because the edited neurons are entangled with many behaviors. A safer, more controlled way is needed to steer how models use contextual vs parametric knowledge without globally altering their internals.
AI.method: The authors propose SHIFT, which replaces hard neuron editing with learnable gate modulation. Instead of permanently changing neuron weights, they attach a lightweight gating module to the frozen backbone model. This module learns to modulate (scale or shift) internal activations during inference, effectively deciding how much to rely on contextual vs parametric knowledge at each step. Training updates fewer than 0.01% of the parameters, leaving the main model untouched. The gate is optimized using supervision from tasks where contextual evidence should override or complement parametric knowledge, so it learns patterns of conflict and appropriate resolutions.
AI.result: On six benchmark datasets designed to test knowledge usage and conflict resolution in RAG, SHIFT outperforms existing baselines, including methods based on neuron editing and other steering techniques. The results show improved alignment with contextual evidence while preserving or better maintaining the model’s general capabilities, indicating that SHIFT can effectively manage knowledge conflicts with minimal parameter overhead.
AI.conclusion: Recasting neuron editing as learnable gating provides a more stable and parameter-efficient way to control how LLMs integrate contextual and parametric knowledge in RAG. SHIFT demonstrates that tiny, external gate modules attached to a frozen backbone can resolve knowledge conflicts more safely than direct neuron manipulation, improving RAG performance across multiple datasets. The framework is practical, lightweight, and publicly released with code and datasets to facilitate further research.

title: Agent-Native Immune System: Architecture, Taxonomy, and Engineering
summary: The transition from static chat bots to autonomous agents--equipped with persistent memory, tool-use protocols, and multi-agent collaboration--has fundamentally expanded the AI threat landscape. Current defense mechanisms, such as perimeter security and training-time alignment, remain external to the agent's active reasoning loop. Consequently, they fall short: a fully aligned agent remains highly vulnerable to runtime hijacking via memory poisoning, tool-chain manipulation, or multi-agent protocol attacks.
